0_2010.description: Collectively, these studies delineate how the composition, genetic capacity, and early-life acquisition of the human intestinal microbiome are shaped by diet, delivery mode, host genetics, mucosal architecture, and antibiotics, and how resulting community states regulate immunity and metabolism to influence risk of inflammatory and metabolic diseases across the lifespan.

0_2011.description: Collectively, these studies elucidate how long- and short-term diet shape gut community composition and function across mammals, how microbial metabolites and host mediators regulate epithelial barrier integrity, immune homeostasis, neural and energy pathways, and how perturbations in these interactions drive intestinal, allergic, autoimmune, and metabolic diseases while revealing therapeutic opportunities through microbial, dietary, and host-targeted interventions.

0_2012.description: This body of work delineates how the composition, function, and stability of the human gut microbiome coevolve with host diet, genetics, development, and environment to modulate metabolic and immune circuits that influence diabetes, metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, steatohepatitis, inflammatory bowel disease, and autoimmunity, while informing targeted interventions including dietary modulation, probiotics, and microbiota transfer.

0_2013.description: This research delineates how dietary exposures remodel the intestinal microbiome and its metabolites to regulate host energy metabolism, mucosal barrier integrity, and immune homeostasis, thereby influencing risks for obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, inflammatory bowel disease, autoimmunity, and malnutrition, with causality supported by gnotobiotic transfers, receptor-mediated mechanisms, and microbial or surgical interventions.

0_2014.description: Collectively, these studies define how host genetics, diet, and daily behaviors shape the composition and function of human microbial communities from early life onward, elucidate metabolite-mediated crosstalk with epithelial and immune circuits that sustains barrier integrity and tolerance, link perturbations to inflammatory, hepatic, metabolic, renal, and perinatal disorders, and inform standardized terminology and targeted microbiome-directed interventions to restore homeostasis.

0_2015.description: This body of work delineates the assembly, spatial organization, and stabilization of the human gut microbiota across the lifespan; elucidates how diet, antibiotics, emulsifiers and other exposures drive dysbiosis that rewires SCFA-GPCR signaling, inflammasome activity, epithelial barrier function and RORγt+ regulatory T cell networks; integrates host genetics and virome alterations; and links these mechanisms to inflammatory bowel disease, metabolic and cardiovascular disorders, asthma, and autoimmunity while informing therapeutic modulation and modeling.

0_2016.description: This body of work elucidates how diet-shaped intestinal microbial communities and their fermentation products—particularly acetate, propionate, and butyrate—regulate epithelial barrier integrity, metabolic and immune homeostasis, and disease susceptibility across development and populations, while revising host–microbe quantification and identifying modifiable determinants and therapeutic avenues.

0_2017.description: This collection delineates how the human gut microbiota—shaped from infancy by diet, prebiotics, and geography—regulates immune, metabolic, and barrier functions, contributes causally to inflammatory, cardiometabolic, and neuroimmune diseases including inflammatory bowel disease, and provides mechanistic targets for precision microbiome-based prevention and therapy.

0_2018.description: This body of work synthesizes evidence that environmental exposures—particularly diet, medications, and early-life transmission—predominate over host genetics in shaping intestinal community composition and function, with downstream effects on barrier integrity, immune regulation, and metabolic outcomes, thereby motivating personalized, ecology-based therapeutic strategies.

0_2019.description: This work integrates longitudinal multi-omics, cellular profiling, and dietary interventions to elucidate how disruptions in gut microbial communities and their metabolites reprogram epithelial and immune circuits to drive disease activity in inflammatory bowel conditions, while nominating fiber-derived short-chain fatty acid augmentation and receptor-mediated signaling as promising therapeutic strategies.

0_2020.description: This compendium delineates how variations in gut microbiome composition and metabolite production regulate mucosal immunity, epithelial barrier function, and systemic metabolism across inflammatory and cardiometabolic disorders, while critiquing taxa-based biomarkers, mapping microbial and virome diversity, assessing antibiotic and drug perturbations and ecosystem resilience, and evaluating dietary and microbe-derived therapies to restore health.

0_2022.description: This body of work integrates multi-omics, causal inference, and interventional evidence to define how diet, antibiotics, environment, and host genetics shape gut microbial ecology and metabolites that regulate barrier integrity, immune and metabolic homeostasis, and disease risk across the lifespan, while advancing microbiome-targeted therapies for inflammatory, metabolic, infectious, and pregnancy-related disorders.

100_2007.description: This research integrates mechanistic, epidemiologic, and exposure-assessment evidence to demonstrate that fine and ultrafine particulate pollution penetrates pulmonary and vascular barriers, activates oxidative and inflammatory pathways that accelerate atherosclerosis, elevates cardiopulmonary morbidity and mortality across spatial and temporal scales, and that refined exposure modeling and emission reductions yield measurable gains in population health and life expectancy.

100_2008.description: This body of research integrates epidemiologic, quasi-experimental, and toxicologic evidence to quantify the adverse impacts of short- and long-term exposure to fine particulate pollution on cardiopulmonary disease, cancer, mortality, and life expectancy, delineates source contributions and spatial variability with land-use regression and global models (including traffic, shipping, and non-exhaust emissions), evaluates susceptible outcomes such as myocardial infarction, heart failure, and adverse birth outcomes, estimates global and regional disease burdens and benefits of reductions, and advances monitoring and indoor–outdoor exposure assessment to inform air quality guidelines and policy.

100_2009.description: This research integrates epidemiology, toxicology, and advanced exposure assessment to show that short- and long-term exposures to ambient and indoor air pollution—particularly fine particulate matter from traffic, shipping, and other sources—increase cardiorespiratory mortality, reduce life expectancy, and contribute to cancer and adverse perinatal outcomes, while advancing source attribution and sensor-enabled monitoring to guide policy.

100_2010.description: This body of work integrates source characterization, spatial exposure modeling, and epidemiology to elucidate how particle size and composition and co-pollutants drive oxidative and inflammatory injury associated with cardiopulmonary disease, cancer, adverse perinatal outcomes, and reduced life expectancy, while evaluating regulatory impacts and sensor-enabled monitoring from indoor microenvironments to global scales.

100_2011.description: This body of research integrates extended cohort analyses, quasi-experimental and spatial studies to demonstrate that chronic and acute exposure to ambient and indoor pollutants—particularly fine particulate matter and related mixtures—elevates cardiopulmonary mortality, reduces life expectancy, and contributes to adverse perinatal and oncologic outcomes, while advancing high-resolution exposure assessment (satellite-derived estimates, land-use regression, sensor networks, trajectory and spatial modeling) and multi-pollutant statistical methods to refine risk quantification, source attribution, and regulatory policy.

100_2012.description: This research synthesizes advances in monitoring and modeling to resolve indoor–outdoor sources, infiltration, and formation of airborne particles and gases, generate high-resolution exposure estimates from buildings to the globe, evaluate meteorological and policy determinants, and quantify consequent cardiopulmonary, oncologic, perinatal, and cognitive risks to inform effective standards and interventions.

100_2013.description: Integrating advanced exposure assessment with source apportionment and mixture modeling, this research quantifies spatially resolved pollutant concentrations and associated risks—spanning mortality, cardiopulmonary morbidity, adverse birth outcomes, and cancer—thereby attributing a substantial global disease burden with socioeconomic disparities and evaluating standards and interventions for effective mitigation.

10_2013.description: Collectively, these studies delineate charge transport and recombination dynamics, compositional tuning, and low‑temperature thin‑film formation in hybrid lead halide absorbers, enabling planar heterojunction devices that exhibit micrometer‑scale diffusion lengths, high mobilities and lifetimes, visible‑range bandgap control, and improved operational stability through interfacial and architectural engineering.

10_2014.description: This collection delineates and advances the structure-property-performance relationships of hybrid halide perovskites by integrating defect and recombination physics, band-gap and compositional tuning, low-temperature solution crystallization, interfacial passivation, device-architecture optimization, hysteresis mitigation, and stability strategies including lead-free formulations, thereby approaching theoretical conversion limits with scalable, high-efficiency photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices.

10_2015.description: This corpus charts advances in halide perovskite photovoltaics and optoelectronics by coupling composition and dimensionality tuning to stabilize photoactive phases, solution processing that yields dense large-grain or single-crystal films with low trap densities, interface and band-alignment design for rapid extraction and suppressed hysteresis, mechanistic insights into ionic/defect migration and moisture, oxygen, and thermal degradation (including reversible hydrates), and exploration of inorganic, mixed‑cation, and lead‑free analogues, collectively enabling scalable, stable devices with certified high efficiencies.

10_2016.description: This research advances metal halide perovskite photovoltaics by integrating compositional and dimensional engineering with interfacial optimization and scalable film processing to achieve efficient, reproducible, and thermally and photochemically stable single-junction and tandem devices, while elucidating degradation mechanisms such as ion and metal migration and exploring lead-free double perovskites.

10_2017.description: This collection elucidates how compositional, dimensional, and interfacial control—including halide management, contact and trap passivation, oriented low-dimensional phases, lead-free variants, strain mitigation, and scalable processing—modulates charge transport and recombination, suppresses environmental and operational degradation, and enables durable, high‑efficiency single‑junction and tandem perovskite photovoltaics.

10_2024.description: These studies focus on molecular and structural engineering of buried interfaces and defect landscapes—using strongly anchored self-assembled contacts, dual-site and amidinium passivants, precursor and crystallization control, and mitigation of ion-induced field screening—to realize highly efficient, scalable, and durable inverted and tandem halide perovskite solar cells.

10_2025.description: The collected studies elucidate how coupling orientation- and composition-controlled absorbers with chemically and mechanically fortified buried interfaces—through self-assembled molecular architectures, two-dimensional buffers, ionic electron shuttles, and lattice reinforcement—suppresses nonradiative and ionic losses to deliver record single-junction and wide-bandgap tandem efficiencies, scalable module stability under heat and humidity, and viable end-of-life recycling pathways.
10_2025.report:
Below is a synthesis across the 2025 papers you provided.

1) What is being researched
- Efficiency scaling
  - Single-junction inverted PSCs approaching/certifying 26–27% PCE; WBG perovskites for tandems with >29% all-perovskite and >34% perovskite–Si tandems.
  - Module-scale performance (cm2 to 785 cm2) with outdoor/diurnal stability.
- Stability and durability
  - Thermal, humidity, and light stability under IEC 61215-like protocols; suppression of ion migration, lattice deformation, and interfacial degradation.
- Buried interface and transport layers
  - Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs), SAM hybrids, chelating/dipolar bridges, ionic-liquid–assisted SAMs, redox-active interlayers; tougher, more adhesive ETLs.
- Perovskite composition and microstructure control
  - Halide/cation homogenization (Cl, Rb), phase-pure α-FAPbI3 without alloying, preferred (100) orientation in WBG films; defect and stress management.
- Scalable processing and manufacturability
  - Wafer-scale 2D buffers (MoS2), vapor-assisted surface reconstruction, uniform SAM deposition on rough FTO, OH-seeding for SAMs, large-area device engineering.
- Sustainability and circularity
  - Aqueous, holistic recycling of perovskite stacks and BoM components; techno-economic and LCA benefits.

2) Key methods
- Buried-interface chemistry
  - Phosphonic-acid and carbazole SAMs with π–π stacking; post-assembled chelating bridges (e.g., CB-PA); interfacial dipole engineering (2DPAA); ionic liquid–SAM hybrids (BMIM-TFSI); redox mediator (methylene blue) at NiOx/perovskite; boric-acid functionalized SAMs to mitigate NiOx corrosion; –OH seeding to homogenize SAM adsorption.
  - Polymerized self-assembled bilayers (Friedel–Crafts crosslinking) for thermal robustness and stronger adhesion.
  - C60-derived ionic salt (CPMACl) as a tougher electron shuttle/ETL; synergistic passivation at perovskite/C60 (mF-PEAI + piperazine iodide).
- Ion migration and lattice reinforcement
  - Wafer-scale monolayer MoS2 above/below perovskite as chemical/physical barriers (Pb–S bonding, type-I alignment).
  - Graphene–polymer (PMMA) reinforcement to raise modulus and suppress photoinduced lattice expansion.
  - Vapor-deposited polydentate ligands to “isolate” defective surface octahedra and stabilize against light–dark cycling.
- Film formation/composition control
  - Iodine intercalation–decalation to kinetically access nonalloyed α-FAPbI3 with high durability.
  - Strain-locked Rb incorporation into α-phase WBG perovskites; alkali-oxalate–assisted homogeneous Cl distribution.
  - Molecular additives (parabanic acid, pyridine-bridged chalcogen-concave, amidinium heterocycles) for concurrent crystallization guidance and defect passivation.
  - Oriented growth (improved (100) texture) in WBG absorbers.
- Scalable interfaces and substrates
  - Face-on, multilayered SAMs with strong π-stacking on rough FTO; ordered hydrophilic SAM bilayers to enable large-area coating.
  - Aluminum glycinate organometallic to couple Me-4PACz/NiOx with perovskite, reduce interfacial stress/defects.
- Recycling/manufacturing
  - Aqueous reprocessing that recovers perovskite, transport layers, electrodes, substrates; demonstrated device re-fabrication with near-fresh performance.

3) Emerging trends (most important)
- Interface-first device design
  - Systematic shift to inverted (p–i–n) with engineered SAMs/dipolar/chelating/redox interlayers that deliver both energy-level control and mechanical/chemical stability.
- Mechanical integrity as a performance lever
  - Adhesion engineering, crosslinked interlayers, and lattice reinforcement (2D/graphene/polymer) to curb thermomechanical and light-driven damage; explicit reporting of adhesion energy, modulus, and interfacial toughness.
- Ion management at scale
  - Dual-side 2D barriers (MoS2), surface octahedra “isolation,” and halide/cation homogenization (Cl/Rb) to suppress migration, phase segregation, and nonradiative loss—critical for WBGs and tandems.
- Manufacturing realism
  - Methods compatible with rough FTO, wafer-scale transfers, vapor treatments, and uniform SAM deposition; device scaling to multi-cm2 and sub-m2 modules with diurnal and outdoor validation.
- Performance under harsh conditions
  - Routine demonstration of 85°C/85% RH damp heat, 85°C MPP, and -40↔85°C thermal cycling with <5–10% PCE loss; progress toward and beyond IEC 61215 benchmarks.
- Sustainability and circularity
  - Holistic recycling strategies with large reductions in resource depletion/toxicity and improved LCOE; early integration of circularity into perovskite PV design.
- WBG/tandem maturity
  - Record-low Voc losses in WBGs, oriented growth, MA-free stacks, and robust interlayers enabling >29% all-perovskite and >34% perovskite–Si tandems; stabilization for perovskite/CIGS tandems.

4) What is new
- Records and validated durability
  - >29% all-perovskite tandems via improved (100) orientation in WBG absorbers; single-junction certified steady-state PCE up to 27.2%; inverted cells certified ≈26.2–26.7% with FF >86%; ≥9.6 cm2 modules at 22.8%; 785 cm2 modules at 19.6% retaining >97% after diurnal cycling; >3,600 h MPP at 90°C with >97% retention via graphene–polymer reinforcement.
- Robust interfacial architectures
  - Covalently crosslinked self-assembled bilayers that survive 100°C/200 h and pass 85/85 (2,000 h) and -40↔85°C (1,200 cycles) with <4% loss.
  - Post-assembled chelating bridges and enlarged/positively oriented interfacial dipoles (≈5 D) to trim energy losses and drive vertical hole transport.
  - Redox mediator–modified SAMs enabling selective redox cycles at buried interfaces with superior operational stability.
  - C60 ionic salt ETL that triples interfacial toughness and delivers ≈26% PCE with minimal thermal/photo degradation.
- Ion and phase control at the source
  - Wafer-scale monolayer MoS2 above/below perovskite as universal passivation/ion-blocking buffers.
  - Strain-induced Rb retention in α-phase WBG perovskites achieving Voc ≈93.5% of radiative limit.
  - Alkali-oxalate strategy to homogenize Cl across films, enabling 27.2% certified steady-state PCE and long MPP stability.
  - Iodine intercalation–decalation route to nonalloyed α-FAPbI3 with >1,100 h operation at 85°C under light.
  - In-situ coherent FAPbIxCl3–x buried interlayers boosting Voc by ~90 mV.
- Scalable processing enablers
  - Ionic liquid–SAM hybrids for smoother, more uniform SAMs and better WBG crystallization; OH-seeding pretreatments for homogeneous SAM adsorption; ordered hydrophilic SAM bilayers aiding large-area perovskite coating with certified ≈25% at ≈1 cm2; face-on SAMs tailored for rough FTO.
- Circular economy advances
  - Aqueous, low-cost recycling recovering perovskite, transport layers, electrodes, substrates; repeated degrade–recycle cycles yielding devices comparable to fresh; up to 96.6% cut in resource depletion and 68.8% lower human toxicity impacts versus landfill.

Overall, 2025 work converges on chemically and mechanically engineered buried interfaces, controlled halide/cation distributions for WBGs, and manufacturing-aware processes that scale high efficiency with proven, standardized stability—while introducing credible recycling pathways to de-risk commercialization.

14_2016.description: This corpus delineates the emission pathways, analytical identification, environmental transport and retention, and biological and food-chain impacts of microplastics and nanoplastics across air, wastewater, freshwater, and marine compartments, quantifying releases from textiles and treatment plants, storage in soils and sediments, uptake and mechanistic toxicity in diverse taxa, and a limited role for chemical transfer, thereby informing risk assessment and mitigation.

14_2017.description: Collectively, these studies quantify global production and disposal of synthetic polymers, elucidate the generation, transformation, and riverine–marine–terrestrial–airborne transport of micro- and nanoplastic particles, map their distributions from urban waters to polar deep-sea sediments, assess trophic transfer and organismal and potential human health effects, advance detection methodologies, and evaluate mitigation via advanced wastewater treatment and regulatory interventions.

14_2018.description: This body of work delineates the definitions, sources and transport pathways, standardized detection and mass-based quantification, contaminant interactions, and ecological and human-health effects of micro- and nanoplastics across aquatic, terrestrial, and food systems—from wastewater, soils, floodplains, sea ice, and ocean gyres to drinking and bottled waters and seafood—including trophic transfer, biotic fragmentation to nanoscale, and alterations to soil structure and microbiomes, thereby clarifying exposure routes and research priorities.

14_2019.description: Collectively, these studies establish a classification framework for small plastic particles, characterize their sources and atmospheric, terrestrial, and aquatic transport and aging, quantify their prevalence in environmental media, biota, foods, and drinking water, evaluate organismal effects and human exposure and health risks, and recommend standardized measurements and mitigation strategies to curb emissions and impacts.

14_2020.description: This corpus investigates the accelerating burden of plastic waste by harmonizing degradation-rate evidence to estimate environmental persistence, delineating transport from terrestrial sources through air and water to remote and deep-sea sinks, documenting organismal and human exposures (including plant uptake and placental occurrence) with emerging toxicological effects, and modeling intervention scenarios to quantify the scale of action required to reduce future emissions.

14_2021.description: This research consolidates evidence on the generation, transport, and persistence of plastic-derived particles across environmental compartments; their weathering and interactions with co-contaminants; impacts on ecosystem functions and biogeochemical cycles; human exposure pathways and health implications; advances in detection and modeling; and mitigation through waste management and circularity-focused policy.

19_2023.description: This research integrates high-field neuroimaging, large-scale recordings, standardized atlases, and graph-based models to map and causally test how structural constraints govern brain-wide communication and functional hierarchies across development and species, yielding open resources that link neural organization to behavior, disease, and artificial intelligence.

19_2024.description: Together, these studies advance an integrative account of brain organization by linking multimodal maps, large-scale recordings, and genetic variation with rigorous computational and theoretical models, providing standardized analysis and correspondence tools, delineating geometric constraints on communication and structure–function relations across the lifespan and disease, and enabling causal, behaviorally anchored interpretations of brain-wide activity.

19_2025.description: This research synthesizes geometric, structural–functional, neuromodulatory, and genetic perspectives to model and map brain-wide network communication across development, behavior, and disease, leveraging multimodal neuroimaging with standardized toolkits and atlases while reciprocally informing brain-inspired computational architectures.
19_2025.report:
Below is a concise synthesis of each paper, followed by cross-cutting emerging trends.

1) Geometric constraints on human brain function
- What is being researched: How geometric properties of the brain (cortical sheet geometry, spatial embedding, wiring constraints) shape functional organization and dynamics.
- Key methods: Geometric/manifold and spectral analyses (e.g., geodesic distance, Laplacian eigenmodes), structure–function modeling on the connectome, generative models constrained by spatial embedding.
- What is new: A geometry-first account of functional patterns, positioning spatial constraints as primary drivers rather than byproducts of connectivity.

2) Neurosymbolic AI: The 3rd wave
- What is being researched: Integration of neural networks (e.g., LLMs) with symbolic reasoning to produce systems that are more reliable, interpretable, and data-efficient.
- Key methods: Differentiable logic, neuro-symbolic program induction, knowledge graphs, constraint solving, tool-augmented LLMs.
- What is new: A coherent “third-wave” framing that operationalizes hybrid learning-reasoning stacks at scale for complex tasks.

3) Brain network communication: concepts, models and applications
- What is being researched: How information propagates across brain networks beyond static connectivity.
- Key methods: Communication models (shortest paths, navigation, diffusion, communicability), network control theory, multistep relay/path ensembles; validation with behavior and pathology.
- What is new: Unifying taxonomy and evaluation of communication models with practical guidance for cognitive and clinical applications.

4) Exploring the frontiers of neuroimaging (review)
- What is being researched: Advances in fMRI, EEG, DTI, TES/tDCS and their application to brain function and disorders (ASD, ADHD, AD, PD).
- Key methods: High-field fMRI, advanced EEG analyses of oscillations, diffusion metrics of connectivity, noninvasive stimulation.
- What is new: Synthesis linking modern acquisition/analysis with translational targets; emphasis on noninvasive modulation as a therapeutic complement.

5) Brain eQTL and network analyses for brain-related diseases
- What is being researched: Genetic regulation of brain gene expression and network-level effects that drive disease risk.
- Key methods: Brain-tissue eQTL mapping, co-expression/network propagation, TWAS-like integration, causal inference frameworks.
- What is new: Convergent identification of downstream effectors and putative causal drivers connecting variants to network and disease phenotypes.

6) CAT: A computational anatomy toolbox for structural MRI
- What is being researched: A comprehensive, QC-rich toolbox for morphometric analysis.
- Key methods: Voxel-/surface-/region-based morphometry, longitudinal pipelines, integrated stats and visualization with GUI and scripting.
- What is new: End-to-end, reproducible morphometry with standardized QC/reporting—lowering barriers for rigorous structural MRI studies.

7) Structure–function coupling in macroscale human brain networks
- What is being researched: The degree and determinants of alignment between structural connectivity and functional connectivity.
- Key methods: Diffusion MRI tractography + resting fMRI; coupling metrics and gradient approaches; predictive modeling.
- What is new: Refined coupling characterizations (including regional heterogeneity and context-dependence) to explain variability across systems and tasks.

8) Cognitive motor dissociation in disorders of consciousness
- What is being researched: Prevalence and detection of covert consciousness in patients lacking overt command-following.
- Key methods: fMRI/EEG mental imagery and command-following paradigms; cross-modality confirmation.
- What is new: Clear prevalence estimates (≈1/4 covert responders without observable commands), reinforcing clinical utility of bedside-compatible EEG/fMRI protocols.

9) Neuromodulatory Control Networks (NCNs) for LLMs
- What is being researched: Dynamic, biologically inspired control over LLM computation at inference time.
- Key methods: Small parallel controllers compute modulatory signals to adjust attention temperature, gains, activation functions; open-source PyTorch implementation.
- What is new: Practical architecture for on-the-fly adaptability, controllability, and mode-switching in otherwise static transformer pipelines.

10) The marmoset brain in stereotaxic coordinates
- What is being researched: A high-resolution reference atlas for marmoset neuroscience.
- Key methods: Stereotaxic coordinate framework, MRI/histology alignment, labeled structures.
- What is new: Standardized coordinates and digital resources to enable precise targeting and cross-study comparability in a key primate model.

11) Human lifespan changes in the brain’s functional connectome
- What is being researched: Age-related trajectories in functional network organization across the lifespan.
- Key methods: Resting-state fMRI across wide age spans; network metrics, gradients, and hub analysis; cross-sectional/longitudinal modeling.
- What is new: Normative curves and region/network-specific aging signatures with implications for early deviation detection.

12) A brain-wide map of neural activity during complex behaviour
- What is being researched: Whole-brain activity patterns underlying naturalistic, multi-faceted behaviors.
- Key methods: Large-scale recording/imaging (e.g., Neuropixels, calcium imaging, fUS/fMRI), behavior annotation, cross-region integration.
- What is new: High-resolution, behavior-resolved brain-wide activity atlas enabling mechanistic tests of distributed control.

13) Network Correspondence Toolbox (NCT)
- What is being researched: Standardized quantification of spatial overlap between user-derived maps and canonical functional atlases.
- Key methods: Dice coefficients with spin-test permutations to assess magnitude and significance of correspondences.
- What is new: Easy, reproducible cross-atlas reporting that improves interpretability and comparability across studies.

Emerging trends (important)
- Geometry-to-function synthesis: Growing emphasis on spatial embedding, cortical geometry, and eigenmodes as first principles for functional organization.
- From connectivity to communication: Shift from static edges to multistep, dynamic communication models and control-theoretic perspectives to explain cognition and symptoms.
- Standardization and tooling: Turnkey, QC-heavy pipelines (CAT) and reporting toolkits (NCT) to raise reproducibility and cross-study comparability.
- Multiscale linkage: Integrating genetics (eQTL), molecular networks, and macroscale connectomics to identify causal chains from variant to network dysfunction.
- Lifespan and covert phenotyping: Normative lifespan connectome models and robust detection of covert consciousness expand clinical utility.
- Brain-wide, naturalistic measurement: Scaling to whole-brain, behavior-anchored datasets in animals and improved primate atlases (marmoset) for translational bridges.
- Neuro-inspired AI architectures: Beyond static transformers—biologically grounded control (NCNs) and neurosymbolic hybrids aim for adaptable, interpretable, and controllable AI.
- Noninvasive modulation: Renewed integration of EEG/MRI with TES/tDCS for closed-loop, precision neuromodulation as diagnostics and therapeutics mature.

What is new across the set
- Conceptual reframing of function via geometric constraints and communication/control models.
- Practical infrastructures that standardize morphometry and network correspondence at scale.
- Convergent, multilevel causal inference linking genes → networks → behavior/disease.
- Demonstrated clinical impact from covert consciousness detection rates and lifespan norms.
- Concrete, open-source proposals to make LLMs dynamically controllable using neuromodulatory principles.

1_2007.description: This collection elucidates how integrated organelle-derived cues, cell-surface perception of elicitors, and hormone-controlled transcriptional circuits coordinate photosynthesis, stress acclimation, innate immunity, and developmental programs in plants, supported by quantitative modeling and high-resolution genomic and interaction assays.

1_2008.description: These papers define an integrated, multiscale framework showing how perturbations of water and ion homeostasis and photosynthetic electron transport under salinity, drought, flooding, heat, and excess light are transduced via redox-controlled signaling, hormone and microRNA crosstalk, osmoprotectant metabolism, and structural barrier and cell wall remodeling to coordinate growth, defense, and recovery, thereby revealing actionable targets for improving plant resilience.

1_2009.description: These studies delineate the molecular architecture of plant stress and immune signaling, showing how pattern-recognition and hormone receptor modules (including ABA receptor–PP2C–SnRK2 control), rapid NADPH oxidase–dependent reactive oxygen species waves, cross-communication with salicylic acid and jasmonate, and transcriptional regulators and small RNAs integrate abiotic and biotic cues to modulate stomatal behavior, gene expression, development, and durable disease resistance.

22_2007.description: This body of work advances convolutional models for visual recognition by integrating principled architectural design of hierarchical features, rectification, pooling and deconvolution with effective training regimes, efficient proposal and context mechanisms, and transfer learning, yielding scalable systems that achieve state-of-the-art performance in classification, detection, and semantic segmentation.

22_2008.description: This body of work traces the evolution from early hierarchical representations to end-to-end convolutional architectures that, combined with proposal mechanisms, advanced pooling, deconvolution, and integrated probabilistic inference, deliver scalable, transferable, and state-of-the-art performance in image classification, object detection, and dense semantic segmentation.

22_2009.description: This body of work traces the evolution from early pixel-level ensemble features to end-to-end trainable hierarchical models that learn and transfer multiscale representations for classification, detection, and dense labeling, introducing advances in architecture design, pooling and normalization, deconvolution, proposal generation, edge and contour integration, and probabilistic refinement that deliver state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency on standard benchmarks.

22_2010.description: This body of work advances visual recognition from efficient low-level representations to end-to-end convolutional architectures that learn and transfer hierarchical features for classification, detection, and dense segmentation, integrating multi-scale pooling, region proposals, and structured inference to deliver state-of-the-art accuracy with markedly improved computational efficiency on standard benchmarks.

22_2011.description: This collection advances image understanding by developing and rigorously evaluating hierarchical convolutional architectures and training schemes—spanning unsupervised representation learning, supervised pretraining and fine-tuning, optimized nonlinearities and pooling, deconvolution and context integration, and region-based inference—to achieve accurate and efficient classification, detection, segmentation, boundary estimation, and proposal generation at scale.

22_2012.description: This body of research systematically advances deep convolutional approaches for visual perception, unifying representation learning, localization, and dense labeling into efficient end-to-end systems that achieve state-of-the-art performance across classification, detection, and segmentation benchmarks.

22_2013.description: This corpus investigates how hierarchical convolutional models, combined with proposal generation, boundary reasoning, contextual cues, and end-to-end training, enable accurate and efficient object localization and pixel-level segmentation, while leveraging transfer learning, weak/semi-supervision, and architectural/computational optimizations validated on large-scale benchmarks.

22_2014.description: This collection advances visual recognition by transferring deep feature representations to region-focused pipelines augmented with spatial pooling, contextual modeling, and fast proposal and edge methods, alongside practical computational speedups, yielding large accuracy gains in detection, segmentation, retrieval, and fine-grained classification on standard benchmarks.

22_2019.description: These papers collectively advance object detection and semantic/instance segmentation by integrating attention-driven context modeling, dynamic and multi-scale feature backbones, anchor-free and cascade detection paradigms, content-aware upsampling, balanced and augmentation-based training, and data-efficient transfer/few-shot strategies, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with reduced computational cost across benchmarks such as COCO, Cityscapes, and LVIS.

22_2020.description: This collection advances object detection and related dense prediction by introducing efficient architectures and operators, anchor-free formulations with adaptive sample selection, geometry-aware localization losses and decoupled heads, transformer-based end-to-end models, specialized solutions for oriented and camouflaged targets, few-shot generalization methods, and new datasets with clarified evaluation protocols.

22_2021.description: This corpus advances visual recognition by developing transformer-centric and attention-enhanced architectures—spanning hierarchical windowed and pyramid designs, multi-scale token fusion, and rotation-equivariant modules—combined with dense self- and semi-supervised training to produce efficient end-to-end models that set state-of-the-art performance in classification, detection, segmentation, and video instance segmentation.

22_2022.description: This collection advances visual recognition by analyzing and integrating convolutional and transformer backbones for detection, segmentation, and video analysis, introducing locality-aware attention, large-kernel designs, self-supervised pretraining, and specialized methods for small, rotated, and fine-grained targets to improve accuracy–efficiency scaling.

22_2023.description: This body of work advances visual recognition by co-designing efficient CNN- and transformer-based architectures, attention and multi-scale fusion mechanisms, and modern training paradigms (self-/semi-supervised, open-vocabulary, and foundation modeling) to achieve state-of-the-art detection and segmentation—especially for small objects and cross-domain UAV/remote-sensing imagery—while optimizing speed, memory, and generalization.

25_2012.description: This research advances electrochemical capacitors by engineering nanostructured carbon and transition‑metal oxide electrodes—including activated and heteroatom‑doped carbons, hierarchical three‑dimensional and binder‑free arrays, and asymmetric device architectures—to optimize ionic/electronic transport and double‑layer/pseudocapacitive charge storage, thereby achieving high energy and power densities with robust cycling in flexible and microscale formats.

28_2022.description: This research synthesizes advances in the regulation and self-regulation of microRNAs, expansion of curated interaction resources and network analytics, and development of high-sensitivity multiplexed and liquid-biopsy assays to enable noninvasive diagnostics, prognostics, and therapeutics in oncology, while underscoring persistent challenges in standardization, specificity, delivery, resistance, and clinical translation.

28_2023.description: This corpus synthesizes advances in microRNA self-regulatory networks, quantitative multiplexed and CRISPR-enabled detection for minimally invasive diagnostics, translational progress and hurdles in therapeutic development and delivery, and expanded curated interaction resources and analytic methods—including machine learning—supporting mechanistic discovery and clinically actionable biomarker panels.

28_2024.description: This collection integrates advances in the biology, trafficking, and regulation of microRNAs with developments in circulating biomarker discovery, assay and biosensor technologies, curated target-interaction resources, and therapeutic design and delivery, delineating current clinical trial progress, challenges, and translational prospects in oncology.

28_2025.description: These studies collectively map advances in microRNA biology and regulation, ultrasensitive isothermal CRISPR-Cas12 detection, circulating biomarker applications for early cancer detection aided by high-throughput profiling and machine learning, the translational progress and challenges of oncology-directed therapeutics, and the expansion of experimentally validated interaction databases that inform mechanism and drug development.
28_2025.report:
Here is a concise, paper-by-paper analysis, followed by cross-cutting emerging trends for 2025.

1) microRNAs in action: biogenesis, function and regulation
- What is being researched: Current understanding of miRNA biogenesis, target recognition, cellular regulation, and roles in physiology and disease.
- Key methods: Synthesis of evidence from high-throughput sequencing, Argonaute/CLIP-based target mapping, loss/gain-of-function perturbations (ASOs, mimics, CRISPR), single-cell profiling, and network analyses.
- Emerging trends: Systems-level views of miRNA–mRNA networks; increasing attention to chemical modifications and context-dependent regulation; integration with disease phenotypes and therapeutic targeting.
- What is new: A consolidated 2025 perspective linking mechanistic layers (processing, editing/modification, localization) with functional outputs and therapeutic implications.

2) microRNA: trends in clinical trials of cancer diagnosis and therapy strategies
- What is being researched: The clinical trial landscape for miRNA-based cancer diagnostics and therapeutics.
- Key methods: Landscape review of registered trials; appraisal of assay platforms (qPCR/NGS panels, liquid biopsy), therapeutic modalities (mimics, antagomirs), and delivery systems (LNPs, conjugates, exosomes).
- Emerging trends: Migration from single-miRNA biomarkers to multi-marker panels; combination with standard-of-care; increasing use of minimally invasive liquid biopsies; growing role of companion diagnostics.
- What is new: Up-to-date mapping of trial phases, target indications, delivery vehicles, and reasons for trial setbacks (toxicity, specificity, dosing), clarifying translational bottlenecks.

3) A one-pot isothermal Cas12-based assay for the sensitive detection of microRNAs
- What is being researched: Rapid, isothermal, CRISPR-Cas12 detection of miRNAs in a single reaction vessel.
- Key methods: One-pot, isothermal CRISPR-Cas12 collateral-cleavage readout for miRNA detection; likely fluorescence or lateral-flow reporting and minimal sample handling (details not provided in abstract).
- Emerging trends: Move toward instrument-light, point-of-care miRNA diagnostics with simplified workflows and faster turnaround.
- What is new: A consolidated, one-pot isothermal Cas12 format specifically tailored to miRNA detection (novelty inferred from title; performance specifics not supplied).

4) Trials and tribulations of microRNA therapeutics
- What is being researched: Barriers and opportunities for clinical translation of miRNA therapeutics, with context from the broader RNA therapy renaissance.
- Key methods: Critical review of preclinical/clinical data on miRNA mimics/inhibitors; analysis of delivery, immunogenicity, specificity, dosing, and off-target risks.
- Emerging trends: Renewed industry interest post-mRNA vaccine success; emphasis on targeted delivery, immune evasion, and rigorous risk/benefit frameworks; manufacturability and cost now central.
- What is new: A 2025-level synthesis underscoring why no miRNA therapy has reached Phase III/FDA approval, with a structured agenda for overcoming safety and selectivity hurdles.

5) microRNAs: circulating biomarkers for the early detection of imperceptible cancers via biosensor and machine-learning advances
- What is being researched: Use of circulating miRNAs for early detection of hard-to-diagnose cancers, leveraging biosensors and ML.
- Key methods: High-throughput miRNA profiling; biosensor platforms; machine learning for signature discovery; integration of multi-omics to improve classifier robustness.
- Emerging trends: Transition from discovery to clinically viable, standardized pipelines; ML-driven multiplex panels; focus on pre-symptomatic/low-tumor-burden detection via liquid biopsy.
- What is new: A focused view on “imperceptible” cancers, articulating how biosensors plus ML/multi-omics can overcome heterogeneity and sensitivity/specificity trade-offs, and calling for protocol standardization.

6) miRTarBase 2025: updates to the collection of experimentally validated microRNA–target interactions
- What is being researched: Expanded, curated repository of experimentally validated miRNA–target interactions (MTIs) and related annotations.
- Key methods: Large-scale literature curation; NLP with LLaMA3 and prompt engineering; network construction (miRNA–mRNA and miRNA–miRNA); UI/data-integration upgrades.
- Emerging trends: AI-assisted curation to scale MTI discovery; linking MTIs to drug response, toxicity, and clinical biomarker roles; attention to oxidative miRNA modifications.
- What is new: Expansion to 3,817,550 validated MTIs from 13,690 articles; inclusion of miRNA–drug interaction data; oxidized miRNA sequence annotations; enhanced network views and a redesigned interface for oncology/drug-development use.

Cross-cutting emerging trends in 2025 (important)
- Clinical translation focus: Strong push to move from discovery to clinically actionable diagnostics and therapeutics, but efficacy, specificity, and safety remain gating factors; no FDA-approved miRNA drugs yet.
- Assay modernization: Rapid, low-infrastructure detection (CRISPR-Cas12 one-pot, biosensors) aims at point-of-care and early detection, especially for low-burden or “imperceptible” cancers.
- Multi-marker and multi-omics strategies: Shift from single miRNAs to multiplex signatures integrated with proteomics/genomics and modeled via ML for robustness and generalizability.
- AI-enabled curation and analysis: Adoption of LLMs (e.g., LLaMA3) to accelerate literature mining, MTI extraction, and association mapping, shortening discovery cycles.
- Network and systems biology: Emphasis on miRNA–mRNA and miRNA–miRNA co-regulatory networks to identify key nodes for intervention and to interpret context-dependent effects.
- Chemical/oxidative modifications: Growing recognition that miRNA modifications (e.g., oxidation) alter targeting and function, now captured in databases and considered in biomarker/therapeutic design.
- Delivery and safety engineering: Lessons from mRNA vaccines drive renewed delivery R&D (LNPs, conjugates, exosomes), with parallel work on immunogenicity control, off-target minimization, and dose optimization.
- Standardization and reproducibility: Calls for harmonized pre-analytical/analytical protocols, reference materials, and clinically relevant endpoints to enable cross-study comparability and regulatory acceptance.

Overall, what is new in 2025 across these works is the combination of: scaled, AI-assisted knowledge bases (miRTarBase 2025); serious movement toward deployable, instrument-light diagnostics; ML-enabled, multi-omics biomarker development for early detection; and a sober, translationally focused reassessment of miRNA therapeutics with clear, prioritized technical hurdles to solve.

29_2024.description: This collection examines how to evaluate, enhance, and deploy contemporary language models—through retrieval and knowledge integration, reasoning-focused prompting, long-context analysis, hallucination detection via uncertainty, and standardized human evaluation—while detailing their opportunities and constraints in clinical and scientific settings to inform safer, more reliable use.

2_2021.description: This collection synthesizes advances from biosynthesis and extraction through analytical evaluation and mechanism-of-action to formulation and delivery of bioactive plant constituents and proteins, addressing stability and bioavailability constraints to translate them into effective agents for disease mitigation and into functional ingredients for next-generation food and cosmetic applications.

2_2022.description: Collectively, these papers delineate how plant-derived bioactives are biosynthesized, extracted, characterized, and mechanistically act, establishing structure–activity relationships and synergistic interactions that underpin demonstrable efficacy in modulating disease processes, preserving foods, supporting skin and cardiovascular health, and advancing functional nutrition and natural product drug discovery through optimized extraction conditions, robust antioxidant assays, and microbiota-mediated metabolism.

32_2023.description: Collectively, these works establish a mechanistic, interface-centric framework—spanning solvation-structure modulation, crystallographic control, corrosion mitigation, functional hosts and separators, and cathode optimization—to achieve highly reversible, dendrite- and side-reaction–resistant zinc operation and durable, high-efficiency aqueous batteries validated at practical areal loadings, lean N/P and electrolyte ratios, wide temperatures, and pouch-cell scale.

32_2025.description: This body of work defines an integrated strategy for aqueous zinc energy storage by tailoring solvation and electric double layer structures, constructing robust organic–inorganic interphases, and coupling anisotropic separators with targeted additives to direct dense dendrite-free deposition, suppress corrosion and parasitic reactions, stabilize multi-electron iodine conversion, and realize long-life, high-efficiency performance across wide temperatures up to pouch-cell scale.
32_2025.report:
1) What is being researched
- Highly reversible aqueous Zn metal anodes: suppressing dendrites, corrosion, and H2 evolution while enabling lean-electrolyte, low N/P, and anode-free operation.
- Electrolyte/interphase co-design: tailored solvation, electric-double-layer (EDL) reconstruction, and in-situ solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation for durable Zn plating/stripping.
- Advanced separators/membranes: high‑modulus anisotropic biodegradable films and quasi–single‑ion COF membranes to stabilize interfaces and raise Zn2+ transference.
- High‑energy Zn–I2 chemistries: stabilizing 4‑electron I−/I0/I+ conversion, mitigating shuttle and I+ hydrolysis, and delivering pouch‑cell performance.
- Cathode architectures/mechanisms: vanadates (ligand‑field modulation, POM intercalation), MnO2 (cation/anion co‑doping, vacancies), sulfide/MXene heterostructures, crystalline–amorphous hybrids, and dissolution control.
- Wide‑temperature operation and practical robustness: −60 to +100 °C cycling, roll‑over stability, Ah‑level pouch cells.
- Data‑guided screening and benchmarking: machine learning for additive design; simple indices to predict corrosion and solvation behavior.

2) Key methods
- Interfacial engineering
  - Water‑deficient interphases via halide‑rich inorganic SEIs and water‑shielding EDLs; bilayer polymer–inorganic SEIs with proton‑holder functionality (e.g., PANI/ZnF2).
  - Helmholtz plane reconstruction: additives that displace “active” water in IHP and reorganize solvation in OHP (e.g., pyridine N‑oxide).
  - In‑situ SEIs from targeted chemistry (e.g., ZnCO3/ZnS via methylurea; halide‑enriched interphases from ionic‑liquid anion–cation synergy).
- Solvation/transport control
  - Co‑solvent electrolytes with “kinetics‑compensation” to raise tZn2+ and weaken ion pairing; dual‑salt hybrids achieving near‑unity CE at moderate molarity.
  - Ionic liquids engineering water‑poor EDLs; crown ethers (18‑crown‑6) to accelerate Zn2+ desolvation; hydrogen‑bond regulators (silk fibroin, sucrose+PAM) to lower water activity and stabilize Zn.
  - Self‑phase‑separated electrolytes: anode‑side nonaqueous phase suppresses HER/dendrites; cathode‑side aqueous phase preserves insertion kinetics.
  - ppm‑level and binary additives (gum macromolecules; saponin+anisaldehyde; lignosulfonate; HMTA; IPA) to tune EDL, suppress HER, and guide deposition.
- Separators/membranes
  - Wood‑inspired anisotropic biodegradable separators coupling high modulus with aligned ionic channels.
  - COF‑Zn quasi–single‑ion membranes (t+ ~0.87, 25 μm) enabling lean electrolyte and wide‑T cycling.
  - Self‑assembled polyelectrolytes forming ion‑separation, fast‑transport channels.
- Zn deposition control and coatings
  - Crystal‑plane shielding and ordered growth from single‑crystal to dense polycrystalline stacking using dual‑phase SEIs.
  - Hydrophobic/electron‑donating and low‑cost coatings (α‑boron nanosheets; fly‑ash silica) to reduce overpotential and ensure uniform nucleation.
- Iodine chemistry
  - Urea chelation stabilizing I+ and unlocking 1.8 V vs Zn/Zn2+ plateau; HMTA protonated species precipitating polyiodides/I+ to suppress shuttle/hydrolysis.
  - Polycationic hydrogels enabling cascade I−/I0/I+ and Br−/Br0 redox with interhalogen stabilization across −50 to 50 °C.
  - Catalysis via Zn single‑atom and dual‑atom sites in hierarchical carbons to accelerate iodine conversion and confinement.
- Cathode design and mechanistic tools
  - Ligand‑field modulation (BTA+ intercalation) to raise redox potential; POM‑induced valence and spacing control to boost kinetics/stability.
  - Dual anion/cation doping, oxygen vacancies, crystalline–amorphous intergrowths (VOx/MXene) to enhance conductivity and mitigate structural strain.
  - In/operando spectroscopy (FTIR, cryo‑EM), XAFS, PDF, FEM, DFT/MD for interphase and mechanism elucidation.
- Screening/benchmarking
  - ML predicting Gutmann donor numbers to pre‑select additives; anionic polarity index (API) to benchmark corrosion at low salt concentrations.

3) Emerging trends (important)
- From “single additive” to holistic interface design: concurrent EDL and SEI co‑engineering that deliberately excludes water from the IHP while ensuring fast Zn2+ transport.
- Practical metrics first: anode‑free Zn cells (near‑unity CE, low N/P), high areal capacity (up to 50 mAh cm−2), lean electrolyte, Ah‑level pouches, roll‑over safety, and extreme‑temperature operation.
- Polarity and phase engineering: ionic specificity (API, donor number) and self‑phase‑separation to satisfy conflicting anode/cathode requirements without resorting to ultra‑high salt.
- Mature iodine chemistry: moving beyond I−/I0 to robust 4‑electron cycling via halogen‑free ligand stabilization (urea) and protonated amines (HMTA), plus catalytic hosts (Zn SA/DA sites).
- Sustainable, low‑cost solutions: biodegradable membranes, bio‑derived/macromolecular additives (silk, saponin, lignosulfonate, gums), and industrial‑waste coatings (fly ash).
- Data‑driven and physics‑guided design: ML‑accelerated additive discovery and compact indices (API) bridging computation with experimental screening.
- Crystallographic control of Zn: enforcing facet‑selective nucleation/growth and mechanically robust interphases to sustain high DoD and current densities.
- Proton management: buffering interfacial pH and deploying “proton holders” while embracing coupled H+/Zn2+ storage pathways where beneficial.

4) What is new
- Trace-additive efficacy: parts‑per‑million additive levels shown to meaningfully extend Zn anode life and suppress parasitics, cutting cost/complexity.
- A quantitative, transferable corrosion metric (API) that predicts and validates low‑corrosion behavior even in low‑concentration salts (e.g., 0.5 M Zn(HBS)2).
- Self‑phase‑separated electrolytes that autonomously form anode‑nonaqueous/cathode‑aqueous zones, delivering long life, low corrosion, and pouch‑cell roll‑over stability.
- Near‑unity Zn CE at moderate concentration (≈3.5 M) via dual‑salt/organic co‑solvent design, enabling 1000+ cycle anode‑free ZN–I2 cells under practical conditions.
- Purpose‑built SEIs: amorphous hybrid ZnCO3/ZnS or halide‑rich interphases formed in situ to create water‑poor IHPs; bilayer SEIs with polymer proton‑holder layers operating at 40 mA cm−2 and −30 °C.
- Direct Helmholtz‑plane reconstruction (e.g., PNO) and deliberate EDL reshaping with synergistic dual additives to harmonize kinetics and stability at high areal capacities.
- Aqueous activation and stabilization of I+ without halogens (urea chelation), achieving a record 1.8 V I+/I0 plateau vs Zn; HMTA‑enabled 4e Zn–I2 in limited‑Zn pouch cells (CE >99% over 1400 cycles).
- Quasi–single‑ion COF separators (t+ ≈0.87, 25 μm) that support lean electrolyte, suppress cathode dissolution, and sustain −40 to +100 °C cycling; wood‑inspired anisotropic, biodegradable separators breaking the modulus–conductivity trade‑off.
- Kinetics‑compensation framework for co‑solvent electrolytes: raising tZn2+ and weakening ion pairs to offset mobility losses, generalizable across solvent families.
- ML‑assisted additive discovery using donor number prediction; rapid identification of high‑DN alcohols (e.g., IPA) that correlate with improved Zn reversibility.
- Ordered Zn electrodeposition from single‑crystal nuclei to dense polycrystals via dual‑phase SEIs; crystal‑plane shielding to bias Zn(002)/(100) growth.
- Robust, scalable coatings and bio‑interfaces (α‑boron nanosheets, fly‑ash composites, saponin/anisaldehyde, gums) that lower overpotential, repel water, and smooth current distribution at low cost.

Overall, 2025 work converges on interphase‑centric, data‑aided electrolyte/separator co‑design that delivers durable, high‑rate, wide‑temperature, and increasingly practical aqueous Zn systems, with iodine chemistries and standardized metrics (API) emerging as key enablers for deployment.

36_2018.description: This research synthesizes and advances network science by developing scalable, theoretically grounded methods for structure discovery, inference, and control—spanning community detection, link prediction, vital-node identification, and signal propagation—while extending from pairwise graphs to multiplex and higher-order representations to capture mesoscopic organization and group interactions across large-scale social, biological, and technological systems.

36_2019.description: This research develops and evaluates methods to uncover and represent network structure—from communities and dimensionality to higher-order group interactions—and elucidates how these features govern and stabilize diverse dynamical processes across social, biological, and technological systems.

36_2020.description: This research advances the foundations and applications of network science by integrating graph and hypergraph modeling, statistical physics, and machine learning to uncover structure, quantify robustness, and predict dynamics—such as contagion, cooperation, and mobility—from large-scale data, while reassessing classical metrics and extending analysis and algorithms to interactions beyond pairs.

36_2021.description: This body of work advances a rigorous understanding of complex systems by extending network modeling to group interactions, demonstrating how topology governs contagion, cooperation, diffusion, and resilience, reevaluating structural measures in context, and applying these insights to human mobility, online platforms, epidemics, and infrastructure planning.

36_2022.description: This research synthesizes theory and data to show how modeling group interactions beyond dyads reshapes the structure, dynamics, resilience, and inference of complex systems—characterizing topology-driven effects on contagion, cooperation, synchronization, and signal propagation; linking socio-spatial behavior and community organization to emergent outcomes; and informing robust planning, decision-making, and sustainability in real-world infrastructures.

36_2023.description: This research advances the modeling and analysis of complex systems with higher-order interactions by unifying hypergraph-based theory, dynamical processes, and large-scale social mobility data to reveal how topology governs contagion, cooperation, community formation, and resilience.

36_2024.description: Integrating random graph theory with higher-order network representations, this research delineates how topology shapes dynamics—encompassing signal propagation, synchronization, epidemic processes, cooperation, consensus, and group decision making—while evaluating robustness, resilience, and deep learning-based community detection across real-world systems.

36_2025.description: This research elucidates how the topology of higher-order networks, formalized through hypergraphs and simplicial complexes and grounded in random graph theory, shapes dynamical processes such as signal propagation, epidemic spreading, robustness, resilience, and group decision making in complex systems.
36_2025.report:
Emerging trends across these works (most important)
- Shift beyond pairwise graphs: Broad adoption of higher-order representations (hypergraphs, simplicial complexes, higher-order Markov models) to capture group interactions driving real-world dynamics.
- Topology-to-dynamics mapping: Systematic use of higher-order spectral/topological tools (Hodge Laplacians, homology/cohomology) to predict diffusion, synchronization, consensus, and contagion regimes.
- Group-effect contagion: Epidemic and diffusion models increasingly include synergistic, nonlinear transmission across groups, changing thresholds and control strategies.
- Robustness rethought: Resilience analyses extend to multilayer and higher-order structures, with interventions targeting groups (simplices/motifs) rather than only nodes/edges.
- Theory consolidation: Local convergence and branching-process viewpoints underpin thresholds, distances, and phase transitions in sparse random networks and their higher-order analogs.
- Data-driven inference: Growing emphasis on inferring genuine higher-order interactions from rich temporal/multimodal data and distinguishing them from aggregated pairwise effects.
- AI-augmented collectives: LLMs are positioned as facilitators, evaluators, and agents in networked group decision-making, raising new methodological and governance challenges.
- Design and control: From “analyze” to “engineer”—using topological levers to shape desired dynamics, improve signal fidelity, and contain cascades in higher-order systems.

Paper-by-paper analysis

1) Title: signal propagation in complex networks
- What is being researched: How network structure modulates transmission, amplification, delay, and distortion of signals across complex networks.
- Key methods: Spectral graph theory (adjacency/Laplacian dynamics), linear response and Green’s functions, motif-based/path expansions, nonlinear simulations for validation.
- What is new: A unifying perspective linking meso-scale features (motifs, communities, redundancy) to propagation fidelity and timing, offering design/robustness guidelines.

2) Title: what are higher-order networks?
- What is being researched: Conceptual foundations, scope, and applications of models that go beyond pairwise relations.
- Key methods: Taxonomy and formal definitions (hypergraphs, simplicial complexes, higher-order Markov chains), comparison criteria, application mapping, pitfalls and best practices.
- What is new: Clarifies terminology and modeling choices; provides guidance on when/why higher-order models are warranted and how to evaluate them in practice.

3) Title: the structure and dynamics of networks with higher order interactions
- What is being researched: Structural properties and dynamical processes on hypergraphs and simplicial complexes across domains.
- Key methods: Generalized Laplacians and Hodge decomposition, higher-order centralities and clustering, generative models, analysis of diffusion/synchronization/consensus/contagion.
- What is new: Integrated synthesis tying empirical evidence to theory; standardized measures and open problems that anchor the field’s next phase.

4) Title: robustness and resilience of complex networks
- What is being researched: Tolerance to failures/attacks and recovery capabilities in complex (including multilayer/higher-order) networks.
- Key methods: Percolation and cascading-failure models, attack/defense strategies, controllability and recovery dynamics, scenario analyses across structures.
- What is new: Extends resilience notions to group-based dependencies and multilayer couplings; highlights interventions targeting motifs/simplices in addition to nodes/edges.

5) Title: random graphs and complex networks (vol. 2)
- What is being researched: Local structure of sparse random graphs; emergence of giant components; distance scaling (small/ultra-small worlds); implications of local convergence.
- Key methods: Local weak convergence, branching-process approximations, configuration and inhomogeneous random graph models; examples from Internet/citation networks.
- What is new: First comprehensive treatment of local convergence and its dynamical/structural implications; connects to modern topics (communities, directed models).

6) Title: epidemic spreading on higher-order networks
- What is being researched: How group interactions reshape epidemic thresholds, prevalence, and control.
- Key methods: Higher-order SIS/SIR models with group transmission terms, mean-field and message-passing on hypergraphs/simplicial complexes, spectral threshold bounds from higher-order Laplacians/tensors, simulations.
- What is new: Reveals regime shifts from synergy and group size/order; introduces control strategies targeting higher-order structures (e.g., immunizing simplices, disrupting co-exposure groups).

7) Title: dynamics of complex systems
- What is being researched: General principles of nonlinear, emergent behavior in complex systems with explicit network and higher-order representations.
- Key methods: Nonlinear dynamics, chaos/criticality, self-organization, agent-based and data-driven modeling linking micro rules to macro phenomena.
- What is new: Updated synthesis integrating higher-order network dynamics and cross-domain case studies, aligning classical complexity theory with modern network science.

8) Title: social network group decision making: characterization, taxonomy, challenges and future directions from an AI and LLMs perspective
- What is being researched: How decisions form and propagate in social networks and how AI/LLMs can support or participate in these processes.
- Key methods: Taxonomy of group decision tasks and influence mechanisms, LLM-mediated facilitation/summarization/argumentation, agent-based simulations, evaluation for consensus quality, fairness, and robustness.
- What is new: Frameworks for LLM-augmented deliberation and consensus; challenge catalog (bias, manipulation, governance, transparency); proposed benchmarks and protocols.

9) Title: topology shapes dynamics of higher-order networks
- What is being researched: Direct links between higher-order topological features and dynamical outcomes.
- Key methods: Hodge theoretic tools (combinatorial/Hodge Laplacians), homology/cohomology, spectral gaps and mode localization analyses; application to diffusion, synchronization, consensus on simplicial complexes.
- What is new: Quantitative predictors tying specific topological invariants to dynamical regimes and phase transitions; actionable design rules for engineering behaviors via higher-order topology.

37_2019.description: This collection advances methodology and software for accurate, scalable, and interoperable first-principles simulations of molecules and materials, uniting new approximations and dispersion treatments with tight-binding and local-correlation schemes, rigorous benchmarking resources, and optimized implementations for both finite and periodic systems on modern heterogeneous architectures.

37_2020.description: These works advance electronic-structure simulation by delivering rigorously validated functionals, dispersion and pseudopotential models, semiempirical tight-binding schemes, and high-performance algorithms integrated into widely used codes, enabling accurate and scalable prediction and analysis of molecular and materials properties, including transport and spectroscopic observables.

37_2021.description: This collection advances and benchmarks electronic-structure theory and its software ecosystem—spanning self-consistent tight-binding with charge-dependent dispersion, modern meta-GGA composite methods, high-accuracy correlated approaches, Wannier/local-orbital projections, and energy-decomposition and real-space bonding indicators—delivering scalable, interoperable tools that enable accurate, efficient prediction of structures, energetics, noncovalent interactions, and spectroscopic and transport properties in molecular and solid-state systems.

37_2023.description: The works report methodological and software advances in electronic-structure theory—including composite approximations, accelerated exchange and integral algorithms, energy decomposition and real-space analyses, and best-practice guidance—validated on extensive benchmarks and applications to molecules and solids.

37_2024.description: This collection advances and contextualizes modern electronic-structure computation by integrating high‑performance, numerically robust software for molecules and solids with innovations in integral algorithms, best‑practice density‑functional protocols, wavefunction and electrostatic‑potential analyses, and energy‑decomposition frameworks, and demonstrates their utility from spectroscopy and materials modeling to pharmaceutical formulation.

37_2025.description: This corpus advances and benchmarks an integrated ecosystem of electronic-structure methods and tools—covering dispersion-informed energy decomposition, multi-representation PAW implementations, high-accuracy coupled-cluster frameworks, wavefunction analysis utilities, and E(3)-equivariant interatomic potentials—demonstrating scalable, validated predictions of interactions, excitations, and properties across molecules and solids with applications to spectroscopy and pharmaceutical formulation design.
37_2025.report:
Below is a concise, paper-by-paper analysis followed by cross-cutting emerging trends.

1) Simple, efficient, and universal EDA based on dispersion-corrected DFT (SOBEDA/SOBEDAw)
- What is being researched: A general, low-cost energy decomposition analysis (EDA) framework at the DFT level and a weak-interaction-focused variant.
- Key methods: Dispersion-corrected DFT; decomposition of interaction energy into physical components; calibration against SAPT; implementation via Gaussian + Multiwfn with a sobeda.sh script.
- What is new: A universal EDA usable for weak interactions, bonds, open-shells, and multi-fragment systems at ~2× a single DFT SCF cost; SOBEDAw yields dispersion/electrostatics ratios consistent with SAPT; turnkey scripting for broad adoption.

2) Turbomole: today and tomorrow
- What is being researched: Capabilities and roadmap of a large-scale quantum-chemistry suite using Gaussian basis sets for molecules to periodic solids.
- Key methods: Robust HF/DFT/embedding/MD; recent additions for molecules and solids; under-development lines include nuclear–electronic orbital (NEO) methods, HF-based adiabatic connection models, simplified TDDFT, relativistic/magnetic properties, and multiscale optics.
- What is new: Consolidated advances (2020–2023), plus a forward-looking plan that broadens excited-state, relativistic, and multiscale modeling with efficiency in focus.

3) Multiwfn: a comprehensive electron wavefunction analysis toolbox
- What is being researched: The latest features and use-cases of a broad wavefunction/density analysis suite.
- Key methods: Analyses of charge distribution, bonding, localization/delocalization, aromaticity, intra/intermolecular interactions, excitations, and response properties; free distribution with extensive tutorials.
- What is new: Systematic presentation of the latest version and representative examples that consolidate Multiwfn as a general-purpose, accessible analysis platform.

4) GPAW: an open Python package for electronic structure calculations
- What is being researched: A PAW-based, multi-representation DFT/GW/BSE/TDDFT code tightly integrated with ASE.
- Key methods: Real-space grids, plane waves, and NAOs connected via real-space transformations; many-body GW/BSE, variational excited states, real-time TDDFT; advanced magnetism; non-linear optical tensors; GPU acceleration via CuPy.
- What is new: Mature multi-basis workflows, expanded excited-state and magnetism capabilities, and practical GPU support with minimal code disruption.

5) The design space of E(3)-equivariant atom-centred interatomic potentials
- What is being researched: Architectural choices and trade-offs for 3D rotation/translation/reflection-equivariant, atom-centered ML potentials.
- Key methods: Group-theoretic equivariance, spherical-tensor features, message passing, body-order controls, and locality/long-range handling strategies.
- What is new: A structured taxonomy and guidance on model expressivity vs. efficiency vs. data requirements, clarifying relationships among state-of-the-art E(3)-equivariant models.

6) Spectroscopic, quantum chemical, and topological calculations of phenylephrine using DFT
- What is being researched: A comprehensive DFT study of phenylephrine’s structure, spectra, and bonding topology.
- Key methods: Ground-state DFT optimizations; vibrational (IR/Raman) and electronic spectra (likely TDDFT); topological analyses (e.g., QTAIM/ELF/NCI); orbital/charge analyses (e.g., NBO).
- What is new: An integrated dataset and assignments for phenylephrine, mapping intramolecular interactions/conformers to spectral signatures.

7) Application of DFT to molecular engineering of pharmaceutical formulations
- What is being researched: A review of DFT-driven design in drug–excipient systems, co-crystals, nanodelivery, and controlled release.
- Key methods: KS-DFT electronic structure; dispersion/π–π interaction quantification; solvation models (e.g., COSMO) for thermodynamics (ΔG); coupling with MM/multiscale workflows for realistic environments.
- What is new: A practical methodology playbook showing DFT-guided co-crystal thermodynamics and pH-responsive release modeling that shortens validation cycles.

8) Overview of developments in the MRCC program system
- What is being researched: Recent advances in a high-accuracy ab initio/DFT suite emphasizing coupled-cluster methods and cost-reduction strategies.
- Key methods: Open-ended CC and CCSD(T); ADC(2); WFT–DFT hybrids; cost reduction via NO/LNO/NAF; embedding; new range-separated double hybrids; accelerations for SCF and analytic gradients.
- What is new: Efficient CBS approaches for CCSD(T); LNO-CCSD(T) extended to open-shells; faster SCF and gradient workflows; expanded excited-state, core-excitation, and ionization treatments; free academic access.

Emerging trends across these 2025 works (important)
- Practical decomposition and weak-interaction modeling at DFT cost: SAPT-like insights via dispersion-aware EDA (SOBEDA/SOBEDAw) without high-level expense.
- Democratization and automation: Turnkey scripting (sobeda.sh), user-friendly toolboxes (Multiwfn), and Python-first ecosystems (GPAW+ASE) lower barriers to advanced analyses.
- Efficiency at scale: Local/canonical acceleration (LNO/NO/NAF in MRCC), simplified excited-state models (sTDDFT), and GPU enablement via high-level libraries (CuPy in GPAW).
- Bridging accuracy and scope: Embedding, WFT–DFT hybrids, and adiabatic-connection models link high-accuracy treatments with larger, realistic systems.
- Excited-state and spectroscopy expansion: Wider coverage of optical properties, real-time TDDFT, BSE/GW, non-linear responses, and core-level states across major codes.
- Equivariant ML potentials maturing: Clearer design guidance balances expressivity, data efficiency, and speed; growing standardization for atomistic simulations.
- Stronger treatment of dispersion and noncovalent interactions: DFT-plus-dispersion and calibrated EDA methods provide reliable component analysis for materials, biology, and formulations.
- Open-shell, magnetic, and relativistic capabilities broaden: Routine access in major packages supports catalysis, spintronics, and heavy-element chemistry.
- Application-driven quantum chemistry: DFT informs co-crystal design, nanocarrier engineering, and controlled-release mechanisms, accelerating formulation R&D via predictive thermodynamics and multiscale coupling.

39_2019.description: This collection delineates how adipose tissue expansion provokes chronic, depot-specific inflammation that disrupts central and peripheral control of nutrient flux to drive insulin resistance and β-cell failure, while identifying therapeutic leverage points—including adipokine and nuclear receptor pathways, innate immune signaling, senescent cell clearance, and GDF15–GFRAL circuitry—to restore metabolic homeostasis.

39_2021.description: This body of work defines how maladaptive adipose expansion and remodeling—driven by disrupted progenitor hierarchies, depot-specific inflammatory and senescent programs, and aberrant lipid partitioning—precipitate systemic insulin resistance and cardiometabolic disease, and highlights therapeutic strategies that restore adipose function and glycemic control.

39_2025.description: This portfolio delineates how adiposity-driven insulin resistance and immune dysregulation orchestrate the onset and progression of diabetes and cardiovascular complications, detailing adipose and endothelial inflammatory circuits and their systemic consequences while appraising lifestyle, pharmacologic, surgical, immunologic, and regenerative interventions to restore metabolic and immune homeostasis.

42_2022.description: Integrating reviews, experiments, and cross-national surveys, this research delineates how pervasive digital platforms and mobile media shape skills acquisition, youth mental health, and civic participation, quantifies benefits and harms from activism to problematic use, documents discrepancies between logged and self-reported exposure, and identifies equity and policy gaps that hinder robust causal inference and effective intervention.

42_2023.description: Integrating systematic, meta-analytic, and qualitative evidence, this research delineates how digital platform design and use—particularly among adolescents—relate to discrepancies between device-logged and self-reported exposure, mechanisms of psychological risk and benefit, and broader implications for social inclusion, civic engagement, and evidence-informed policy and prevention.

42_2024.description: This research examines how pervasive digital media use influences adolescent mental health and self-representation and reshapes civic communication and governance, identifying risk and protective mechanisms, inequalities in access and skills, and evidence-based levers for prevention and policy.
