45_2018.description: This research integrates continuously closing global reconstructions, deforming-plate kinematics, standardized geochronology, petrological–thermodynamic modeling, and geodetic constraints to quantify how plate boundary reorganizations since the breakup of Pangea have governed ocean basin evolution, continental deformation, and transcrustal magmatic systems, with calibration from regional orogens and comparative planetary samples.

45_2019.description: This research synthesizes deformable plate kinematic reconstructions and geodetic constraints with mineralogical–geochemical chronometers from terrestrial and lunar samples to elucidate the mechanisms of lithospheric assembly and destruction, transcrustal magma storage and eruption, and their spatiotemporal expression in orogenic systems and associated geohazards.

45_2020.description: This corpus advances a unified view of lithospheric evolution by reconstructing continental breakup and assembly since the Triassic, quantifying distributed deformation and trench migration, documenting craton destruction and orogenic growth across China, Tibet, and the Mediterranean, cataloging volcanism and present-day strain and hazards, and extending comparative constraints to planetary bodies with new lunar and asteroid samples.

45_2021.description: This corpus synthesizes deep-time to present geodynamic research by integrating deforming plate reconstructions since the Triassic, kinematic and structural analyses of the Mediterranean and Himalayan–Tibetan orogens, GPS-constrained strain fields in China, comprehensive Holocene volcanism and hazard assessments, and petrological–geochemical characterization of returned lunar (Chang’e‑5/6) and asteroid (Bennu) materials, complemented by advances in visualization, thermophysical modeling, and mineral nomenclature, to refine quantitative models of tectonomagmatic evolution, volatile reservoirs including natural hydrogen, and associated risks.

45_2022.description: This collection integrates terrestrial and planetary geoscience—encompassing crustal deformation, lunar and asteroid sample characterization, and subsurface hydrogen degassing—with advances in visualization, standardized mineral nomenclature, and phase-change modeling, complemented by studies on morphosyntactic alignment and the historical development of China.

52_2018.description: This research synthesizes advances in numerical analysis and machine learning to produce robust, energy-stable, and efficient models for complex fluid dynamics and coupled multiphysics, encompassing turbulence closures, compressible and reacting flows, stabilized convection–diffusion transport, gradient-flow integrators, multiphase particulate reactivity, electromagnetic wave propagation, and fluid–structure vibration, with validation on canonical instabilities and deployment in modern open-source frameworks.

52_2019.description: This body of work advances theory, computation, and data-driven methods for predictive modeling of complex fluid flows and coupled fluid–structure systems, spanning turbulence and instability-induced mixing, compressible and reacting regimes, energy-stable gradient-flow discretizations, reduced-order and surrogate modeling, and model calibration and updating, with applications to hypersonic propulsion, heat-transfer enhancement in wavy ducts, particulate reacting flows, and fluid-transporting flexible pipelines.

52_2020.description: This body of work develops and integrates physics-based numerical methods with data-driven and reduced-order strategies to deliver stable, efficient, and uncertainty-aware prediction and control of multiscale continuum systems, spanning heat and mass transport, turbulent and compressible flows up to hypersonic conditions, reactive processes, and coupled fluid–structure dynamics.

52_2021.description: This body of work consolidates and advances computational mechanics for fluids and coupled continua by introducing energy-stable discretizations, reduced and hyper-reduced models, and efficient multiphysics solvers across convection–diffusion, gradient-flow, compressible, turbulent, non-Newtonian, and reactive particulate regimes, rigorously verified within modern software frameworks and applied to thermal transport, flow control, and fluid–structure dynamics.

52_2022.description: This body of work advances predictive computational mechanics by integrating modern finite element and CFD formulations, transmission-line and spectral techniques, and reduced-order and surrogate models to efficiently and robustly simulate shocks and transonic phenomena, turbulence in non-Newtonian and reacting multiphase flows, structural dynamics with model updating, and anisotropic electromagnetic propagation in complex engineered systems.

55_2018.description: These studies synthesize the scientific, engineering, and economic foundations for deploying hydrogen in low‑carbon energy systems, comparing renewable production routes, solid‑state and molecular storage and delivery options, supply‑chain and seasonal storage design, and applications in mobility and industry, while delineating constraints in efficiency, cost, safety, and material reliability.

56_2018.description: This research synthesizes evidence on the occurrence and sources of metallic contaminants in soils and waters, their trophic transfer and health risks via crop systems, plant uptake and tolerance mechanisms at molecular to whole-plant scales, quantitative indices for contamination assessment, and the comparative performance of physicochemical, microbial, and plant-based interventions for prevention and remediation to protect food safety and public health.

57_2018.description: This body of work advances power conversion topologies, synchronization and virtual-inertia mechanisms, impedance-based modeling, and hierarchical coordination to ensure stability, power quality, and efficient operation in low-inertia grids, enabling reliable microgrid integration and control from dc distribution to multiterminal transmission.

57_2019.description: This corpus synthesizes advances in AC/DC microgrids and converter-dominated power systems—covering step-up conversion, grid-forming and synchronization control, harmonic and transient stability, distributed optimization and energy management, and supporting modeling tools—to enable reliable, efficient operation under high renewable penetration and low inertia.

57_2020.description: This body of work synthesizes advances in modeling, control, and energy management for microgrids and power electronics dominated grids, with a focus on stability across time and frequency scales (low inertia, harmonic, synchronization, and transient), grid-forming inverter behavior and protection, and practical implementation from planning and optimization to peer-to-peer operation.

57_2021.description: This research advances the analysis, design, and coordination of grid-forming converter controls—encompassing synchronization, damping-based frequency regulation, transient and small-signal stability, and overcurrent limiting—to ensure secure operation of low-inertia, renewable-dominated power systems and microgrids.

57_2022.description: This body of work examines how high penetrations of converter-interfaced resources reshape power-system dynamics, advancing stability characterization, synchronization and current-limiting controls for grid-forming and grid-following inverters, and optimization-based planning and operation of AC/DC microgrids for resilient, economical integration under uncertainty.

57_2023.description: This corpus surveys and advances the modeling, control, protection, and operational optimization of converter-dominated electric power systems across local and bulk scales, emphasizing unified interfacing architectures and dualities, current limiting and fault ride-through, stability from small-signal to transient and subsynchronous regimes in weak conditions, seamless transitions between autonomous and interconnected operation, and optimization-based energy management under uncertainty with storage and responsive demand.

57_2025.description: This collection investigates planning, control, and restoration of microgrids and converter-rich power systems, integrating decentralized regulation, dynamic economic dispatch and demand response under uncertainty, inverter current limiting and stability characterization, and advanced optimization to improve stability, resilience, and cost–emission performance across AC/DC and multi-energy networks.
57_2025.report:
Here is a concise cross-paper synthesis.

1) What is being researched
- Microgrids: architectures, classifications, standards, and especially decentralized/secondary control, energy management and scheduling, and resilience/restoration under uncertainty.
- Optimization for operation: dynamic economic dispatch, multi-objective scheduling (cost–emissions–degradation), robust and distributionally robust formulations, and integration of demand response (price-elastic and integrated DR).
- Converter-dominated grids: grid-forming inverter behavior, current limiting under faults, and updated stability notions for high-IBR (inverter-based resource) systems.
- Power electronics and control: single- and multi-phase inverter control (repetitive and quasi-resonant controllers), coordinated control for large-scale wind/PV/storage.
- Data- and physics-informed methods: physics-informed neural networks, improved random forest models, and AI/ML for system identification, calibration, and scheduling.
- Multi-energy systems: coupling electricity–heat, storage, energy sharing, and market-based coordination in restoration.
- Advanced motor control: sensorless control and flux-weakening techniques for PM and synchronous reluctance machines.

2) Key methods
- Distributed and decentralized control: droop-based and observer-based secondary control; dynamic consensus observers; operation over directed communication graphs; Lyapunov-stability-backed designs.
- Event/self-triggered and hybrid-triggered control to cut communication, sampling, and computation rates while maintaining stability/performance.
- Optimization:
  - Distributed algorithms for dynamic economic dispatch (initialization-free).
  - Risk-averse, two-stage stochastic and distributionally robust optimization for uncertain renewables and disaster impacts.
  - Market-aided coordination (Cournot–Nash bidding) for multi-energy microgrids in restoration.
  - Meta-heuristics and quantum-inspired algorithms (QPSO, swarm variants) for multi-objective EMS.
- Converter and inverter control:
  - Current-limiting strategies for grid-forming inverters; graphical performance comparisons.
  - Equivalence-based design between repetitive controllers and multiple quasi-resonant controllers; frequency-robust tuning.
- Data-driven/AI:
  - Physics-informed networks for motor operating curve identification from minimal/no-load data.
  - Improved random forest for T&D parameter optimization.
- Standards and system perspectives: IEEE MG operation/control standards; harmonized perspectives on large-scale power electronics integration.

3) Emerging trends (important)
- Shift to decentralized, communication-efficient secondary control in DC/AC microgrids (event/self-triggered and hybrid-triggered), emphasizing scalability, privacy, and reduced overhead.
- Resilience by design: risk-averse scheduling and market-aided restoration for multi-energy systems under extreme weather, with explicit modeling of line fragility and renewable uncertainty.
- Reframing stability for converter-dominated grids: practical focus on grid-forming inverter current limiting, fault ride-through, and post-fault recovery as system-level determinants, with growing standardization.
- Co-optimization of economics and sustainability: explicit inclusion of emissions, storage degradation, and trading revenues in EMS objective functions; rising role of price-elastic and integrated demand response.
- Unification of controller design: mapping equivalence across repetitive and quasi-resonant controllers to streamline tuning and robustness.
- Minimal-calibration, physics-informed identification replacing expensive test benches; online or lightweight experiments for drives and power components.
- Multi-energy microgrids with energy sharing and coordinated electricity–heat control moving from concept to operational frameworks.
- Increasing use of distributionally robust and hybrid meta-heuristic algorithms for practical, constraint-heavy MG scheduling.
- Hardware-in-the-loop and real-network validations becoming standard for control proposals.

4) What is new
- Initialization-free distributed algorithm for dynamic economic dispatch in microgrids, removing feasibility-sensitive starts and improving practicality for time-varying conditions.
- Hybrid-triggered, observer-based secondary control for multi-bus DC microgrids over directed networks, jointly optimizing communication, sampling, and computation; stability guaranteed and experimentally validated.
- Physics-informed neural network that identifies MTPA and flux-weakening curves from simple no-load acceleration data, eliminating calibration test benches across diverse IPMSM/SynRM types.
- Comprehensive, performance-visualized review of current-limiting strategies for grid-forming inverters, clarifying trade-offs among fault current, voltage support, stability, and recovery and aligning with evolving standards.
- Formal equivalence relations between repetitive controllers and multiple quasi-resonant controllers for single-phase inverters, enabling new robust-tuning procedures and simplified MQRC design; experimentally verified on a 1.5 kW platform.
- Market-aided, risk-averse restoration for multi-energy distribution systems under typhoon uncertainty: stress–fragility-based outage modeling, Cournot–Nash microgrid bidding, and a privacy-preserving distributed “alternating search” algorithm; validated with real typhoon data.
- Distributionally robust scheduling for community integrated energy systems with integrated demand response, handling renewable uncertainty at the distribution/community scale.
- Quantum particle swarm optimization for grid-connected microgrids achieving simultaneous cost and CO2 reductions with quantified gains and robustness across configurations, illustrating the promise of quantum-inspired meta-heuristics.
- Sensorless control for five-phase flux-intensifying PM motors with space decoupling and an adaptive frequency complex-coefficient filter SMO, boosting torque under flux-weakening and improving position estimation.
- Improved random-forest-based resistance reduction method for T&D systems (TEE case), pointing to practical ML-assisted infrastructure optimization.
- State-of-the-art reviews consolidating microgrid EMCs, decentralized control, meta-heuristic optimization, and power-electronics-based grid integration, plus updated stability frameworks for high-IBR systems, offering reference baselines and research roadmaps.

59_2024.description: Together, these papers elucidate how selective, micro-, and macroautophagic pathways are coordinated by nutrient- and energy-sensing networks and cargo receptors to maintain proteostasis, detail how their dysfunction drives aging, neurodegeneration, cancer progression, immune escape, and skeletal pathology, and outline therapeutic strategies that restore or exploit lysosomal degradation, including targeted protein disposal and immunomodulatory interventions.

59_2025.description: This collection synthesizes how autophagy and its regulatory pathways orchestrate cellular and tissue homeostasis in cancer, skeletal biology, and neurodegeneration, elucidates links between energy-stress signaling and lysosomal dysfunction in disease, and evaluates pharmacologic, biomaterial, and immune-modulatory strategies to therapeutically manipulate this process.
59_2025.report:
Scope of the 2025 papers
- Cross-cutting focus on autophagy and the autophagy–lysosome system in disease: cancer biology and immune escape, neurodegeneration and aging, bone metabolism/regeneration, and core energy-stress signaling (AMPK) and autophagy genes.

1) What is being researched
- Cancer and immunity: How autophagy and related pathways (mTOR–AMPK–ULK1–VPS34, TFEB/TFE3; canonical and non-canonical autophagy) support tumor survival, shape antigen presentation, modulate cGAS–STING signaling, and drive resistance/sensitivity to immunotherapies.
- Neurodegeneration and aging: Mechanisms of autophagy–lysosome dysfunction across neurons and glia; the bidirectional feedback among proteinopathy, senescence, aging, and impaired autophagic flux.
- Bone biology: Roles of autophagy in osteoblasts, osteoclasts, osteocytes, chondrocytes, mesenchymal cells, vascularization, and its contribution to fracture repair, osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, and intervertebral disc degeneration; therapeutic modulation via drugs and biomaterials.
- Core signaling and genetics: Redefinition of AMPK’s role in initiating and sustaining autophagy under energy and stress cues; disease links of autophagy genes beyond the core ATGs.

2) Key methods
- Cellular and molecular autophagy readouts: LC3 puncta and tandem LC3 flux reporters (GFP/mCherry-LC3), p62/SQSTM1 turnover, bafilomycin/lysosomotropic chase assays, mitophagy reporters (e.g., mt-Keima), lysosomal pH and membrane permeabilization assays (LysoSensor, galectin-3 puncta), EM/CLEM.
- Genetics and screening: Conditional ATG knockouts/knock-ins, CRISPR KO/CRISPRi/a and pooled screens for autophagy and lysosome regulators, interactomics/proximity labeling (BioID/APEX) of autophagy complexes.
- Systems biology: Single-cell and spatial transcriptomics/proteomics to map cell-type and state-specific autophagy; metabolomics and lipidomics to link selective autophagy (mito-, lipo-, ferritinophagy) to metabolism.
- Disease models and platforms: iPSC-derived neurons/glia, patient-derived organoids, mouse models of neurodegeneration, cancer, fracture/osteoporosis/OA/IVDD; in vivo autophagy reporters; tumor–immune co-cultures and syngeneic models for immunotherapy combinations.
- Therapeutic modulation: mTOR inhibitors, AMPK activators, ULK1/VPS34 modulators, lysosome inhibitors; phytochemicals; engineered biomaterials/nanomaterials with tunable surface chemistry/shape/roughness to induce autophagy in bone regeneration.

3) Emerging trends (important)
- Context-aware autophagy modulation: Disease-, stage-, and cell-type–specific strategies acknowledging autophagy’s dual roles (tumor-protective vs tumor-suppressive; neuroprotective yet stress-impaired; bone regeneration–supportive).
- Selective and non-canonical autophagy as targets: Prioritizing pathway- and cargo-specific interventions (mitophagy, lipophagy, ferritinophagy; LC3-associated phagocytosis/endocytosis; secretory autophagy) over blanket inhibition/activation.
- Immune interface: Autophagy as a central regulator of tumor antigen presentation, STING signaling, cytokine/chemokine landscapes, and checkpoint therapy response; rational combinations with immunotherapies to overcome resistance.
- Lysosome-centered therapeutics: Targeting lysosomal biogenesis/function (TFEB/TFE3), trafficking, and acidification as leverage points for neurodegeneration and cancer.
- AMPK spatial signaling: Lysosome-localized, compartmentalized AMPK control of autophagy initiation and organelle quality control; integration with mTORC1 at the lysosome and stress cues beyond ATP depletion (calcium, ROS, hypoxia).
- Biomaterials-autophagy axis in orthopedics: Using nano/bioengineered materials to program autophagy for bone healing and degenerative joint/disc diseases.
- Autophagy–senescence–aging loop: Recognition that impaired autophagy promotes neuronal/glial senescence, which further suppresses autophagy, creating a pathogenic feedback that is temporally targetable.
- Toward precision biomarkers: Development of autophagy state signatures (transcriptomic/proteomic/functional) to stratify patients and guide when to inhibit or enhance autophagy.

4) What is new
- Cancer and immunotherapy: Consolidation of autophagy’s bidirectional effects on immune escape and therapy efficacy, with a clearer framework for when to inhibit vs potentiate autophagy in tumors or immune cells to enhance checkpoint blockade and other immunotherapies.
- Neurodegeneration/aging: Stronger mechanistic linkage among disease-associated proteins, aging, senescence, and impaired autophagic clearance, highlighting timing and cell-type precision as prerequisites for effective autophagy-based interventions.
- Bone metabolism: Expanded evidence that all major bone-resident cells and angiogenesis use autophagy in regeneration; therapeutic promise of mTOR/AMPK modulators, phytochemicals, and autophagy-tuning biomaterials/nanomaterials with clinically relevant design parameters.
- AMPK in autophagy: A reframed view of AMPK as a spatially organized, lysosome-anchored regulator coordinating autophagy initiation, lysosomal programs, and selective organelle turnover, beyond a simple energy sensor.
- Autophagy genes and pathways: Broadened disease mapping beyond core ATGs to include lysosomal and trafficking genes, and increased emphasis on selective autophagy receptors/adaptors as druggable nodes.

Net takeaways
- The 2025 literature pivots from “autophagy on/off” to precision, context-specific control that exploits selective and non-canonical pathways, integrates immune and metabolic crosstalk, and leverages materials science—aiming for combinatorial, stage-aware therapies across cancer, neurodegeneration, and bone disease.

66_2019.description: This body of work systematically maps the applications, impacts, and governance of artificial intelligence in contemporary education, delineating advances in personalization, tutoring, and assessment alongside challenges in pedagogical integration, empirical rigor, educator and learner preparedness, and ethics.

66_2020.description: This corpus synthesizes and evaluates how artificial intelligence is reshaping teaching, learning, and assessment across educational contexts—particularly through conversational agents, adaptive systems, and analytics—while exposing gaps in pedagogy, ethics, literacy, and teacher trust that inform priorities for design, practice, and policy.

66_2023.description: This corpus examines how large language model chatbots are reshaping teaching, learning, and assessment by synthesizing early evidence on benefits for personalization and efficiency, documenting risks to accuracy, ethics, and academic integrity, and outlining pedagogical, policy, and operational frameworks for responsible adoption across educational contexts.

66_2024.description: This corpus investigates the deployment of generative systems in education—especially higher education—by cataloging applications and adoption determinants, measuring effects on performance, self-regulation, and higher-order cognition, identifying risks such as technological dependence, bias, and inequity, and proposing literacy, design, assessment, and governance frameworks for responsible implementation.

67_2018.description: This body of research investigates how climate variability and long-term warming—including heat, drought, and excessive rainfall—are altering global agricultural productivity and food security, and evaluates mitigation, adaptation, and system-redesign pathways across crops and livestock (spanning improved management and adoption, dietary change, sustainable intensification, controlled-environment production, agrivoltaics, and integrated water–energy–food approaches), with region-specific assessments that underscore constraints and priorities in Sub-Saharan Africa.

68_2021.description: This collection advances a quantitative, observation- and model-constrained understanding of atmospheric particles by integrating improved ground and satellite retrievals with mechanistic studies of formation, composition, and acidity to refine radiative and cloud effects, attribute dust sources, evaluate emission-driven air-quality responses, and assess health impacts including prenatal exposure, thereby informing mitigation strategies and helping explain recent warming linked to reduced maritime emissions.

68_2022.description: Collectively, these studies refine constraints on atmospheric composition by integrating ground-based and satellite observations with thermodynamic, laboratory, and modeling advances to resolve aerosol acidity and microphysics, mineral dust source contributions, nitrogen dioxide retrieval accuracy, intervention-driven air-quality changes, and the resulting impacts on clouds, radiation, and recent climate variability.

68_2023.description: This body of work integrates coordinated ground validation, enhanced satellite retrievals, machine-learning attribution, and global modeling to refine atmospheric measurements, quantify the impacts of pandemic lockdowns and maritime fuel regulations on air quality, constrain regional and seasonal dust contributions, elucidate particle nucleation and growth alongside gas–aqueous partitioning, and assess the resulting radiative and circulation consequences evident in the recent abrupt warming.

71_2024.description: An integrated assessment of contemporary high-energy and nuclear physics unifies precision collider measurements, heavy-hadron spectroscopy, thermal and perturbative field theory, and advanced simulation and detector developments to refine global averages, map hadronic and proton structure at the percent level, and sharpen searches for departures from established predictions.

71_2025.description: This collection charts coordinated advances in fundamental-interaction studies, uniting record-precision lepton dipole moment determinations, comprehensive heavy-flavor averages, heavy-ion probes of deconfined matter, forward-region collider programs, and upgraded reaction/transport simulations with modern gauge and effective field theory to sharpen quantitative tests of established theory and constrain potential new physics.
71_2025.report:
Executive takeaways
- Precision frontier is driving discovery potential: record-precision measurements (muon g−2, electron g, electron EDM) are shrinking phase space for new CP violation and TeV–PeV-scale BSM physics, with progress dominated by systematic control, long coherence times, and rigorous field metrology.
- Data consolidation and forward instrumentation: global averages (PDG, HFLAV) and new facilities (FPF) are central to extracting weak effects, constraining PDFs/CKM, and opening forward/TeV-neutrino and long-lived particle discovery channels.
- Theory/computation maturation: improved hadronic inputs for g−2, effective field theories for heavy hadrons/molecules, and versatile transport/reaction codes (PHITS, TALYS) are tightening links between theory, phenomenology, and applications (medical, space, environment).

Paper-by-paper analysis

1) Measurement of the positive muon anomalous magnetic moment to 0.20 ppm (Fermilab Muon g−2)
- What: Precision determination of aμ = (gμ−2)/2 using 2019–2020 data; combined FNAL result reaches 0.20 ppm; new world average at 0.19 ppm.
- Methods: Storage-ring technique; measurement of anomalous precession frequency ωa and magnetic field map ω̃′p via NMR; improved beam stability, field uniformity, and beam-dynamics corrections; larger positron sample.
- Emerging trends: Systematics-limited precision metrology; integration of beam-dynamics modeling with field mapping; growing tension/consistency tests with evolving SM theory inputs.
- What’s new: >4× statistics vs 2018 result; >2× smaller systematics; world-average precision improved by ≈2×.

2) Averages of b-hadron, c-hadron, and τ-lepton properties (HFLAV, to Apr 2021)
- What: Global averages of branching fractions, lifetimes, mixing, CPV, semileptonic parameters, and CKM elements.
- Methods: Rescaling to common inputs, treatment of correlations, global fits/BLUE-style combinations.
- Emerging trends: Increasing reliance on correlated global fits to resolve flavor tensions (e.g., lepton universality, |Vcb|/|Vub|); precision inputs for LHCb/Belle II era.
- What’s new: Consolidated post-2020 dataset to early 2021; consistent framework enabling updated CKM and rare-decay constraints.

3) PHITS v3.33: recent improvements
- What: Upgrades to a general-purpose Monte Carlo particle/heavy-ion transport code up to ~1 TeV energies.
- Methods: Enhanced nuclear data library compatibility; improved track-structure algorithms; integrated utilities (PHIG-3D viewer) and RT-PHITS for radiotherapy.
- Emerging trends: Convergence of radiation transport with life/medical sciences; fine-grained track-structure modeling for microdosimetry and space/health applications.
- What’s new: Recommended track-structure modes for specific regimes; packaged visualization and clinical workflows.

4) TALYS: modeling of nuclear reactions
- What: Comprehensive nuclear reaction modeling across wide energies/isotopes.
- Methods: Optical-model potentials; direct + pre-equilibrium + compound (Hauser–Feshbach) mechanisms; level-density/γ-strength systematics.
- Emerging trends: Unified modeling pipelines feeding evaluations, astrophysics, and applications; uncertainty quantification growing in importance.
- What’s new: Consolidated methodology reference; aligns with modern evaluation and application needs.

5) Improved bound on the electron’s electric dipole moment (eEDM)
- What: New most-stringent upper limit on eEDM; result consistent with zero; ≈2.4× improvement.
- Methods: Trapped molecular ions with huge intrinsic E-fields; coherence times up to 3 s; spin-precession readout with strong systematics rejection.
- Emerging trends: Quantum-control platforms (molecular ions, long coherence) pushing CPV searches beyond collider reach; systematic-error engineering dominates progress.
- What’s new: Order-of-seconds coherence; strongest constraints on broad BSM CPV at multi-TeV to PeV-effective scales.

6) Gauge/string duality, hot QCD and heavy-ion collisions (book, reissued OA)
- What: Holographic methods applied to QGP properties, transport, jet quenching, quarkonium.
- Methods: AdS/CFT duality; finite-T/density holography; comparison to RHIC/LHC and lattice QCD.
- Emerging trends: Holography as a qualitative benchmark for strongly coupled transport.
- What’s new: Open-access availability broadening reach.

7) Finite-temperature field theory (book, reissued OA)
- What: Formalism and techniques for QFT at high T/μ; applications to QED/QCD/EW, astrophysics, cosmology, heavy-ion.
- Methods: Functional integrals, HTL resummation, lattice, phase transitions/nucleation.
- Emerging trends: Systematic resummation and EFT frameworks foundational for hot QCD and early-universe modeling.
- What’s new: Open-access reissue.

8) Foundations of perturbative QCD (book, reissued OA)
- What: Systematic treatment of factorization, resummation, and TMDs.
- Methods: Rigorous proofs and phenomenology connecting to collider data.
- Emerging trends: TMD/Q0-resummation central to precision LHC/ep programs.
- What’s new: Open-access reissue.

9) Measurement of the electron magnetic moment
- What: Most precise elementary-particle property; g/2 = 1.00115965218059(13) with 0.13 ppt uncertainty.
- Methods: Single-electron Penning trap spectroscopy with quantum-jump detection; comparison to high-order QED SM prediction requiring α.
- Emerging trends: Synergy between precision spectroscopy and fundamental constants; resolving α inconsistencies is the bottleneck.
- What’s new: 2.2× improved precision; implies α−1 prediction with 0.11 ppb if theory adopted.

10) The Forward Physics Facility (FPF) at the HL-LHC
- What: Proposed underground facility near ATLAS to study forward physics: TeV neutrinos, PDFs, non-perturbative QCD, and BSM LLPs.
- Methods: Shielded caverns; suite of specialized detectors; simulations of forward fluxes/backgrounds; civil engineering studies.
- Emerging trends: Colliders as high-energy neutrino sources; forward physics bridging collider and astroparticle domains.
- What’s new: Matured concept with infrastructure/detector designs and physics case consolidated.

11) Chiral perturbation theory for heavy hadrons and chiral EFT for heavy hadronic molecules
- What: EFT frameworks for heavy-hadron interactions and molecular exotics (e.g., X/Y/Z states).
- Methods: Heavy-quark symmetry + chiral symmetry; power counting; coupled-channel scattering; lattice/experiment constraints.
- Emerging trends: Molecular interpretation of many exotics; EFT tools standardizing predictions/uncertainties.
- What’s new: Unified treatment linking heavy-hadron χPT and molecular EFT.

12) Review of Particle Physics (PDG 2024)
- What: Global compendium of particle/cosmology data and reviews; 2,717 new measurements from 869 papers.
- Methods: Data vetting, averaging, limits; extensive topical reviews; digital/print dissemination.
- Emerging trends: Rapid iteration of global fits; integration of collider, neutrino, cosmic datasets.
- What’s new: Updated 2024 world averages/reviews; optimized mobile/web tools and app.

13) Introductory Nuclear Physics (textbook)
- What: Foundational reference spanning nuclear structure, reactions, and applications.
- Methods: Pedagogical synthesis of theory/experiment.
- Emerging trends: Continues to underpin training for applied/precision nuclear work.
- What’s new: Reference utility; not a new research result.

14) The ALICE experiment: a journey through QCD (comprehensive review)
- What: Synthesis of ALICE Run 1–2 results in Pb–Pb, Xe–Xe, p–Pb, pp; QGP properties and small-system collectivity.
- Methods: High-granularity tracking and PID; flow measurements, jets, heavy flavor, strangeness, femtoscopy.
- Emerging trends: QGP-like signatures in small systems; precision tomography of QGP with heavy flavor/jets; synergy with astrophysics/QCD few-body physics.
- What’s new: Consolidated interpretation and upgrade prospects for Run 3–4.

15) The anomalous magnetic moment of the muon in the Standard Model (update)
- What: Current-theory status of aμ(SM) with hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP) and light-by-light (HLbL) contributions.
- Methods: Data-driven dispersive HVP using e+e−→hadrons; lattice-QCD inputs; refined HLbL calculations.
- Emerging trends: Convergence tests between lattice and data-driven HVP; theory uncertainty now dominated by hadronic inputs.
- What’s new: Updated inputs and uncertainties setting the benchmark for comparison with new Fermilab results.

16) Lost in Math / Existential Physics (book review)
- What: Critique of aesthetics-driven theory-building and reflection on science’s limits vis-à-vis metaphysics.
- Methods: Philosophical/epistemic analysis of particle physics culture.
- Emerging trends: Growing emphasis on testability, precision, and diversified discovery strategies (precision/astrophysical/frontier facilities).
- What’s new: Contextual lens on why precision and novel experimental avenues are ascendant.

72_2024.description: Integrating population burden estimates, mechanistic pathways (inflammatory, autonomic, and glymphatic), objective digital phenotyping, and randomized evaluations of behavioral and device-based therapies, this research delineates the heterogeneity of disordered sleep and its links to cardiometabolic and neurocognitive outcomes to inform precision clinical and public health strategies.

74_2018.description: This body of work elucidates how extracellular matrix mechanics and fluid cues are integrated by adhesion-based molecular clutches, cytoskeletal architecture, and phase-separated signaling assemblies to govern force transmission, signaling, and migration from single cells to tissues, with consequences for development, immunity, regeneration, and cancer.

74_2019.description: These studies elucidate how tissue and fluid mechanics are detected at intercellular and matrix interfaces to reconfigure the cytoskeleton and organelles, reprogram transcription, and thereby control migration, invasion, morphogenesis, immune function, epithelial turnover, and malignancy, while introducing methods and materials to measure and modulate these processes across model systems.

74_2020.description: These studies delineate how integrin-centered cell–matrix adhesions and associated cytoskeletal and nuclear mechanosensors transduce extracellular mechanical cues into transcriptional, metabolic, and migratory programs that govern development, immune function, and cancer progression, while advancing measurement, biomaterial, and therapeutic strategies to interrogate and modulate these pathways.

74_2021.description: These studies elucidate how cell–matrix adhesion systems, the cytoskeleton and the nucleus decode physical cues such as stiffness, viscoelasticity and fluid viscosity to govern three-dimensional migration, metabolic programs and immune–cancer crosstalk, thereby informing quantitative assays and targeted interventions.

74_2022.description: These papers delineate how cells transduce extracellular mechanical cues through cytoskeletal architecture, adhesion complexes, organelle-based sensing, and mechanosensitive channels to regulate adhesion, migration, metabolism, and immune function in three-dimensional tissues, revealing therapeutic opportunities to limit invasive disease.

74_2023.description: Collectively, these studies delineate how integrin-centered adhesion complexes and actin dynamics transduce diverse tissue mechanical cues—encompassing matrix architecture, viscosity, and luminal forces—into signaling programs that govern directed migration, immune regulation, and cancer dissemination in three-dimensional contexts, while advancing in vivo force measurements and therapeutic strategies that modulate these pathways.

74_2024.description: Together, these studies define how tissue physical cues—ranging from matrix architecture and stiffness to fluid viscosity—are transduced by integrin adhesions and force-gated ion channels to rewire actin and signaling programs that regulate cell migration, immune function, and cancer progression, while advancing ligand-guided targeting and in vivo force sensors to modulate and quantify these mechanobiological pathways.

74_2025.description: This body of work delineates how integrins and allied mechanosensors transduce three-dimensional matrix cues to govern cytoskeletal architecture, migration, immune–tissue interactions, and extracellular matrix remodeling, reveals vulnerabilities such as disulfide stress–induced cytoskeletal collapse, and advances translational strategies from ligand-directed targeting to in vivo force-resolving measurements.
74_2025.report:
Scope of research
- How cells sense, interpret, and remodel mechanical cues via integrins, the actin cytoskeleton, and Piezo channels across 3D environments and in vivo.
- How extracellular matrix (ECM) composition, architecture, and mechanics regulate immunity, migration/invasion, and disease progression (cancer, fibrosis).
- Therapeutic exploitation of integrin pathways and RGD–integrin interactions for targeted delivery and mechano-signal modulation.
- Redox–mechanical coupling as a cell-death vulnerability (disulfidptosis) centered on actin/adhesion integrity.
- Development of tools to quantify forces in living tissues (e.g., intraluminal force dynamics).

Key methods
- Advanced 3D systems: organoids and cells in tunable, fibrous/viscoelastic hydrogels with controlled ligand display (e.g., RGD), porosity, and stress-relaxation; 3D traction force microscopy and force-inference in fibrillar matrices.
- Molecular mechanobiology: integrin activation/clustering assays; live-cell super-resolution imaging of adhesions; FRET/DNA-based integrin tension sensors; optogenetic or chemogenetic control of Rho GTPases/talin/kindlin; CRISPR perturbations of integrins and adaptors (talin, kindlin, tensin, FAK/Src, ILK).
- Redox and cytoskeleton: redox proteomics/biorthogonal labeling to map disulfide bonds on actin/actin-binding proteins; high-content imaging of actin architecture and adhesion failure under disulfide stress.
- In vivo mechanics: upconverting luminescent microgauges for real-time mapping of intraluminal pressures/shear; intravital imaging; microfluidics for controlled shear/pressure.
- Systems “matrisomics”: decellularized ECM proteomics integrated with single-cell and spatial omics to define ECM–cell niches and immune–matrix interactions.
- Therapeutic engineering: activation-state–selective integrin ligands, RGD-functionalized nanoparticles/biologics, bispecifics, protease/pH-activated prodrugs; combination regimens with immunotherapy and anti-fibrotics.

Emerging trends (most important)
- From 2D to 3D/in vivo mechanobiology: consensus that 3D, fibrous, and dynamic ECMs reshape adhesion architecture, force transmission, and signaling; new in vivo-compatible force sensors (upconverting microgauges) close the measurement gap.
- Immuno-matrix convergence: ECM stiffness, fiber alignment, and composition actively pattern immune cell localization and function; immune cells reciprocally sculpt the matrisome—positioning ECM modulation as an immunotherapy co-target.
- Precision integrin therapeutics: shift from broad blockade to state-/context-selective targeting (activation-state ligands, multivalency/avidity control) and delivery (RGD-guided carriers) to reduce off-tumor effects and overcome pathway redundancy.
- Mechanometabolic vulnerabilities: disulfidptosis links disulfide stress to actin/integrin collapse; mechanical load and ECM context modulate redox lethality—suggesting combinatorial mechano–redox therapies.
- Piezo–integrin crosstalk: mechanical gating of Piezo channels integrates with adhesion/cytoskeletal tension to control proliferation, migration, and inflammation, expanding the mechanotherapeutic target space.
- ECM as a therapeutic variable: engineering viscoelasticity and plasticity (not just stiffness) to direct cell fate; targeting crosslinking (e.g., LOX), fibrillogenesis (fibronectin/collagen), and matrix remodeling (MMP/ADAMTS) to reprogram diseased tissues.
- Matrisome-guided stratification: spatially resolved ECM signatures (“matreotypes”) emerge as predictors of invasion, immunotherapy response, and drug delivery efficacy.

What is new (paper-linked highlights)
- Targeting integrin pathways: updated therapeutic frameworks emphasize activation-state–specific ligands, adaptor interface targeting (talin–kindlin–integrin), and combination strategies that co-modulate ECM mechanics.
- Actin cytoskeleton vulnerability to disulfide stress mediates disulfidptosis: delineates a cell-death mode driven by disulfide crosslinking within actin/adhesion machinery; positions cytoskeletal redox state and ECM attachment as exploitable liabilities, especially in high SLC7A11 contexts.
- Cell–ECM mechanotransduction in 3D / Cellular mechanotransduction in health and diseases: codify 3D-specific adhesion structures, force propagation in fibrous/viscoelastic matrices, nuclear mechanotransduction, and disease links; provide guidance on translating 2D findings to physiologic 3D settings.
- The extracellular matrix and the immune system: formalizes bidirectional ECM–immune regulation; calls for integrating matrisomics with spatial immunology to map mechano-immune niches and therapeutic windows.
- Mechanisms of assembly and remodeling of the ECM: clarifies force-dependent fibronectin/collagen fibrillogenesis, crosslinking, and long-range fiber alignment; underscores feedback between cell-generated tension and matrix architecture in pathology.
- Mechanisms of mechanotransduction and physiological roles of Piezo channels: synthesizes structural/biophysical advances with system-level roles (vascular tone, fibrosis, sensation); points to emerging modulators and Piezo–adhesion interplay.
- RGD peptide in cancer targeting: surveys benefits/limitations; spotlights solutions such as multivalent/avidity-tuned carriers, activation-state–specific RGD designs, and microenvironment-responsive conjugates to improve tumor selectivity and delivery.
- The role and regulation of integrins in cell migration and invasion / Integrin and its associated proteins as a mediator for mechano-signal transduction: integrate adaptor mechanics (talin/kindlin/tensin), catch-bond behavior, and downstream YAP/TAZ/FAK signaling with invasive programs; propose adaptor interfaces and mechano-checkpoints as drug targets.
- Upconverting microgauges reveal intraluminal force dynamics in vivo: introduces minimally invasive, photostable, background-free force reporters enabling spatiotemporal mapping of luminal pressures/shear (e.g., gut, airways, vasculature), bridging benchtop mechanics with real physiology.

75_2018.description: This research advances ultrathin, lightweight, and flexible electromagnetic-wave shielding and absorption materials by engineering MXene- and carbon-based composites through controlled architectures, surface/defect modifications, and polymer integration to optimize conductivity, impedance matching, and absorption-dominant performance across practical frequency bands while ensuring mechanical robustness and multifunctionality.

75_2025.description: This collection advances lightweight electromagnetic attenuating and shielding materials by multiscale interfacial and architectural engineering of carbon–magnetic and MXene systems—via heteroatom/defect optimization, strain and Schottky modulation, hollow and ordered architectures, and layered gradients—to balance impedance and loss for broadband, angle-tolerant absorption with tunable performance, while integrating thermal management, corrosion resistance, mechanical robustness, antimicrobial activity, sensing, and radar–terahertz–infrared stealth.
75_2025.report:
1) What is being researched
- High‑efficiency electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorption and electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding materials spanning GHz–THz–IR bands, with emphasis on lightweight, flexible, and multifunctional composites.
- Interface- and defect-engineered hybrid systems (carbon/magnetic, conductor–semiconductor, heteroatom‑doped) to optimize impedance matching and interfacial polarization.
- Hierarchical architectures (0D/1D/2D/3D; hollow, yolk–shell, core–shell, ordered arrays, layered gradients) to couple multiple loss mechanisms and broaden bandwidths.
- Application-oriented functions: thermal management, infrared camouflage, corrosion resistance, hydrophobicity/antimicrobial, mechanical robustness, and angular/polarization stability.
- Scalable manufacturing and structural programmability (DIW/DLP 3D printing, wet spinning, field‑aligned assembly) for adaptive performance.

2) Key methods
- Heterointerface engineering
  - Schottky contacts, p–n heterojunctions, and built‑in electric fields (BIEFs) to intensify interfacial polarization and charge separation (e.g., NiS/Co3S4/NiCo@CNTs/NC; Sb‑hybridized CoS2/Sb2S3@CNFs; conductor–semiconductor stacks).
  - Heteroatom/dopant control (N, O, Sb) to tune electronic structures and polarization relaxation (e.g., Fe2N@Ni3Fe/CNFs).
- Hierarchical structural design
  - Hollow/yolk–shell and porous aerogels to increase multiple reflections and reduce density; coaxial nano‑cables (AgNW@C); cactus‑like BNNS/Co@NCNTs for decoupled thermal/EM pathways.
  - Ordered/anisotropic assemblies (1D magnetic microchains; 2D ordered polyhedral arrays) for low‑frequency response and angular insensitivity.
  - Layered/gradient architectures guided by absorption–reflection–transmission power coefficients to spatially separate impedance matching and loss functions (Ni@MXene gradients).
- Magnetic–dielectric synergy
  - MOF‑derived carbon/magnetic hybrids (Co/Fe/Ni ferrites, nitrides, selenides QDs) supported on CNTs/CNFs/MXene/graphene to balance complex permittivity/permeability.
- Processing and integration
  - Electrospinning, solvothermal/pyrolysis of MOFs, plasma‑assisted rapid annealing (strain in TMDs), ice templating, molten‑state reduction.
  - Additive manufacturing: DIW/DLP‑printed aerogels with architected lattices; wet‑spun PVA–MXene fibers; Janus membranes and twin‑coated aerogels.
- Modeling/characterization
  - DFT for interfacial bonding/electronic states; finite‑element simulations of EMI mechanisms; RCS simulations; A–R–T power‑coefficient analysis.

3) Emerging trends (most important)
- Multispectral compatibility and adaptive control
  - Single materials achieving radar–THz–IR stealth and thermal insulation (AgNW@C aerogels; MXene/ANF films with emissivity ~0.097).
  - Dynamically tunable EMI/EM absorption via compression, orientation, humidity, light/electric stimuli (DIW honeycomb MXene/Fe3O4 aerogels; multimodal MXene textiles).
- Interface‑dominated loss mechanisms
  - Purposeful construction of BIEFs, Schottky barriers, and p–n junctions as primary levers for polarization relaxation and bandwidth broadening; reviews consolidate principles and roadmaps.
- Gradient and anisotropic meta‑architectures
  - Functionally graded, layered designs to reconcile impedance matching and attenuation; ordered/anisotropic arrays and chain‑like magnets for low‑frequency, angle‑robust response.
- Thermal–EM co‑design
  - “Direction‑decoupling” networks enabling simultaneous high thermal conductivity and broadband absorption; integration of phase‑change matrices and joule‑heating elements for thermal management.
- Additive manufacturing and structural programmability
  - DIW/DLP for precise lattices with on‑demand EMI and reversible thermal transport; wet spinning for durable, flexible EMI textiles.
- Practicality and durability
  - High‑temperature MAMs via multiscale design; corrosion resistance, hydrophobicity, mechanical robustness; biomass/low‑cost precursors (wood, chitin, egg‑derived carbons).
- Pushing low filler, thin‑layer, and low‑freq performance
  - Ultra‑broad EABs at ~2 mm thickness and stable oblique/polarization response indicate maturing design rules for real‑world constraints.

4) What is new (highlights)
- Performance and bandwidth
  - Multiple‑Schottky hybrid (NiS/Co3S4/NiCo@CNTs/NC): RLmin ≈ −75.9 dB (2.6 mm).
  - Hollow engineering with CoFe2Se4 quantum dots: EAB ≈ 11.4 GHz at 20 wt% loading.
  - Gradient magnetic‑MXene laminates guided by A–R–T coefficients: full X‑band coverage at ~2.0–2.2 mm; RLmin ≈ −68.7 dB.
  - Long‑chain 1D magnetic microchains aligned with E‑field: RLmin ≈ −66.4 dB with 0–90° oblique stability.
- Multispectral and adaptive systems
  - Coaxial AgNW@C aerogel: GHz RL ≈ −66.5 dB; THz SEavg ≈ 71.9 dB with >50 dB RL; IR emissivity ≈ 0.28 with strong thermal insulation.
  - DIW‑printed MXene/Fe3O4/AgNW aerogels: SE up to ~80 dB tunable by compression; humidity‑switchable k from ~0.08 to 0.67 W·m−1·K−1; reversible IR stealth.
  - ANF/MXene‑K+ double‑network films: EMI ≈ 72 dB, emissivity ~0.097, joule heating at 1 V with fast response.
- Interface physics as design axis
  - Sb‑hybridization to build p–n heterojunctions with internal fields for enhanced polarization loss (RLmin ≈ −57.5 dB at 2 mm).
  - Conductor–semiconductor heterointerface polarization enhancement across diverse stacks; reviews on BIEFs and single‑atom strategies codify mechanisms.
- Structural innovations
  - Cactus‑like decoupled thermal/EM pathways (BNNS + vertically grown Co@NCNT arrays): EAB ≈ 6.7 GHz with in‑plane k ≈ 2.55 W·m−1·K−1.
  - 2D ordered polyhedral arrays and plasma‑strain‑engineered TMDs for interfacial coupling and tunable dielectric response.
  - Alternating MXene/Co sandwiches with “frosted” Co to prevent stacking/agglomeration, improving RCS and IR stealth simultaneously.

Overall, 2025 work moves from “material-by-material” improvements to interface‑ and architecture‑centric engineering, achieving thin, lightweight, multifunctional, and tunable EM protection across multiple spectra, backed by simulation‑guided design and scalable fabrication.

76_2020.description: This research integrates controlled synthesis with interface and defect engineering to modulate excitonic, dielectric, and ferroic responses in atomically thin semiconductors and their heterostructures, approaching intrinsic transport and Schottky–Mott junction limits and enabling wafer-scale integration of low-resistance contacts and high-performance, ultrabroadband, polarization-sensitive photodetectors and transistors.

76_2021.description: This body of work investigates scalable synthesis, electronic structure control, and emergent interlayer phenomena in two-dimensional materials and van der Waals heterostructures, enabling optimized contacts and wafer-scale integration and delivering polarization-sensitive, self-powered broadband photodetectors and infrared imaging spanning ultraviolet to mid-infrared wavelengths.

76_2023.description: This body of work advances scalable epitaxy, interface and defect engineering, and heterostructure design of two-dimensional transition metal chalcogenides to realize silicon-compatible transistors and ultrabroadband, self-powered, room-temperature photodetectors, while uncovering interfacial ferroelectricity and polarization-sensitive responses spanning the ultraviolet to mid-infrared.

77_2018.description: Collectively, these studies delineate how oncogenic signaling, nutrient availability, and stromal interactions drive adaptive metabolic reprogramming that sustains growth, metastasis, and immune evasion, while revealing context-specific vulnerabilities that can be therapeutically targeted to enhance treatment and immunotherapy efficacy.

77_2019.description: These studies delineate how lactate- and glutamine-centered metabolic reprogramming sustains tumor energetics and biosynthesis, sculpts an acidic nutrient landscape that suppresses immunity, drives metabolic heterogeneity, and reveals actionable vulnerabilities for precision therapy.

79_2018.description: These works elucidate how atomic-scale active-site design and surface/interface engineering across copper-based, single-atom, bimetallic, tandem, and molecular catalysts, coupled with flow-cell optimization, operando mechanistic analysis, and techno-economic benchmarking, enable stable, high-rate, and selectively controlled electrochemical conversion of carbon dioxide to multicarbon products such as ethylene and alcohols toward scalable deployment.

81_2018.description: This synthesis integrates advances on mangrove extent, loss drivers, carbon storage, and restoration efficacy; nutrient management to curb algal blooms from lakes to coasts; and climate-driven shifts in ocean carbon export and oxygenation, leveraging remote sensing, long-term records, and whole-ecosystem experiments to inform conservation and water-quality policy.

81_2019.description: An integrated assessment of anthropogenic alteration of aquatic biogeochemistry that links ocean deoxygenation, acidification and nutrient enrichment to shifting planktonic and macroalgal blooms, evaluates iron- and particle-mediated carbon export, and quantifies the distribution, losses, gains and carbon stocks of coastal wetlands—particularly mangroves—via global remote sensing to inform resilience-based conservation under rising sea levels.

81_2020.description: This research synthesizes observational, experimental, and remote-sensing evidence showing that nutrient enrichment, warming, acidification, and sea-level rise are intensifying algal blooms and deoxygenation from lakes to coasts, altering ocean carbon sequestration and threatening fisheries and public health, while high-resolution mapping reveals dynamic losses and gains of tidal wetlands—especially mangroves—whose conservation and restoration regulate coastal carbon storage and resilience.

81_2021.description: This research synthesizes global evidence on how nutrient enrichment and climate forcing are transforming aquatic ecosystems—intensifying freshwater and marine algal blooms, reshaping ocean biogeochemical cycling, and driving loss and recovery of mangrove and tidal wetlands—while advancing remote sensing, modeling, and management to protect water quality, biodiversity, and carbon sequestration.

81_2022.description: Collectively, these studies quantify and explain recent global changes in coastal and inland waters by integrating high‑resolution remote sensing, long‑term event records, and biogeochemical analyses to separate human and natural drivers of mangrove and tidal wetland change, reassess harmful bloom trends under variable monitoring, and evaluate climate–nutrient controls on carbon storage, ecosystem services, and public health to inform targeted conservation and restoration.

81_2023.description: Collectively, these studies delineate how nutrient enrichment and climate forcing reshape inland-to-coastal waters by intensifying harmful algal blooms and altering wetland and mangrove dynamics, while advancing global observation and experimental frameworks to quantify risks to health and ecosystem services and to inform carbon-focused conservation and restoration.

81_2024.description: This collection integrates global observation and mechanistic evidence to quantify the expansion and intensification of phytoplankton blooms in inland and coastal waters, resolve multi-decadal trajectories of intertidal wetland and mangrove loss and gain, elucidate feedbacks governing carbon sequestration and carbonate formation, and assess monitoring and restoration strategies to bolster ecosystem resilience under accelerating environmental change.

81_2025.description: Integrating global assessments and mechanistic analyses, this research quantifies three centuries of wetland loss, maps mangroves at 10-m resolution, compares restoration pathways for maximizing coastal carbon sequestration, elucidates nutrient-driven controls behind the expansion and toxicity of phytoplankton and cyanobacterial blooms in inland and marine waters, and advances remote-sensing and in situ monitoring to guide scalable mitigation and conservation.
81_2025.report:
Synthesis across the set
- What is being researched
  - Status, trajectories, and resilience of coastal wetlands (mangroves, tidal marshes, tidal flats), including their blue carbon potential and conservation/restoration pathways.
  - Expansion/intensification of phytoplankton and harmful algal blooms (HABs) in marine and inland waters, their drivers (eutrophication, climate change), and risks.
  - Quantification and mapping advances: historical wetland loss over centuries; global, high-resolution mangrove extent; global monitoring of cyanobacterial pigments (phycocyanin).
  - Biogeochemical efficiency: environmental controls on converting nutrients to chlorophyll in lakes and implications for monitoring and management.

- Key methods
  - Multi-sensor remote sensing at global scale (high-resolution optical and radar for mangroves; ocean color for marine blooms; inland-water algorithms for phycocyanin).
  - Time-series trend analyses and climate–biogeochemical modeling/forecasting.
  - In situ automated sensing, metagenomics, and toxin analytics for HABs.
  - Comparative assessment of restoration strategies; blue carbon accounting and MRV (measurement, reporting, verification).
  - Historical reconstructions combining archives, inventories, and backcasting from modern observations.

Emerging trends (most important)
- From area to quality: conservation shifting from simply protecting extent to enhancing ecosystem condition, sediment supply, species interactions, and multi-scale resilience.
- Operational, high-resolution Earth observation: 10 m global habitat maps; global phycocyanin products; near–real-time bloom detection via satellite–sensor fusion and AI.
- Standardization and MRV: calls for reference materials and harmonized protocols for cyanotoxin quantification; rigorous blue carbon MRV enabling policy/finance integration.
- Restoration prioritization: evidence-based preference for reforestation (restoring former mangroves) over afforestation for higher carbon additionality, survival, and co-benefits.
- Coupled climate–eutrophication framing: warming, stratification, and altered circulation amplifying nutrient-driven blooms; management integrates watershed controls with in-system, nature-based and advanced treatments.
- Mechanistic interpretation of proxies: refining how chlorophyll is used as a proxy for biomass/productivity by quantifying environmental controls on nutrient-to-chlorophyll yield.
- Multi-scale governance: aligning local restoration with regional sediment/nutrient regimes and global climate targets to secure long-term wetland persistence and services.

What is new (paper-specific highlights)
- Extensive global wetland loss over the past three centuries
  - New long-term, global quantification of wetland loss since the 1700s, setting historical baselines for restoration targets.
  - Methods: synthesis of historical land-use records with modern remote sensing and modeling to reconstruct trajectories.

- Coastal phytoplankton blooms expand and intensify in the 21st century
  - Evidence of geographic expansion and increased intensity/duration of coastal blooms under recent climate and nutrient regimes.
  - Methods: satellite ocean-color time series linked with climate/oceanographic drivers.

- Eutrophication: causes, consequences, and mitigation techniques
  - Integrative review spanning physical, chemical, and biological mitigation, positioning trade-offs and deployment contexts.
  - Methods: comparative assessment of legacy nutrient management, in-system treatments, and nature-based solutions.

- Mapping global distribution of mangrove forests at 10-m resolution
  - First global mangrove map at 10 m, enabling finer-scale management, change detection, and MRV.
  - Methods: high-resolution optical (and likely radar) classification with machine learning and tidal/topographic corrections.

- Mangrove reforestation provides greater blue carbon benefit than afforestation
  - Comparative analysis showing higher carbon gains, permanence, and ecological suitability from reforestation of former mangrove areas.
  - Methods: field and remote-sensing carbon stock/flux estimates combined with accounting of survival and site suitability.

- Harmful algal blooms in inland waters
  - Synthesis of inland HAB drivers, risks, and management gaps; emphasizes detection/forecasting needs in lakes/reservoirs.
  - Methods: review of monitoring (sensors, satellites), modeling, and intervention strategies.

- Blue carbon and the role of mangroves in carbon sequestration
  - Comprehensive overview of sequestration mechanisms, estimation methods, human impacts, and conservation/economic instruments.
  - New emphasis on linking biophysical processes to incentive structures and policy.

- Cyanobacteria harmful algal blooms: causes, impacts, and risk management
  - Calls for standardized cyanotoxin analytics and reference materials; advocates AI-enabled in situ sensing for routine monitoring.
  - Methods: risk assessment framework integrating exposure routes, public-health implications, and monitoring technologies.

- Harmful algal blooms in eutrophic marine environments: causes, monitoring, and treatment
  - Cross-ecosystem comparison of monitoring (remote sensing, autonomous sensors, modeling, metagenomics) with control options (physical, chemical, biological, advanced tech).
  - New framing of cost–efficacy–impact trade-offs to guide integrated, context-specific mitigation.

- Environmental controls on the conversion of nutrients to chlorophyll in lakes
  - Clarifies how light, temperature, mixing, and community composition modulate chlorophyll yield from nutrients, refining proxy use.
  - Methods: meta-analysis and/or mechanistic modeling linking nutrient loads to chlorophyll variability.

- Coastal wetland resilience through local, regional and global conservation
  - Framework highlighting scale-dependent threats and resilience drivers; argues for quality-focused, multi-scale conservation.
  - Methods: synthesis across biophysical and social drivers with actionable conservation priorities.

- Monitoring phycocyanin in global inland waters by remote sensing: progress and future developments
  - State-of-the-art on global phycocyanin retrievals; advances in atmospheric correction for turbid waters, cross-sensor harmonization, uncertainty quantification.
  - Roadmap toward operational, standardized cyanobacteria monitoring products.

Key methods distilled
- Remote sensing: 10 m habitat mapping; ocean color for bloom phenology; inland-water algorithms for phycocyanin.
- In situ/omics: autonomous fluorometers, toxin assays, metagenomics for community/toxin profiling.
- Modeling/AI: statistical and process-based forecasts; machine learning classification/detection; sensor fusion.
- Carbon accounting: stock/flux measurements, additionality/permanence analyses for blue carbon MRV.
- Historical reconstruction: integrating archives with modern observations to set baselines.

Overall, these papers collectively move the field toward operational, standardized, high-resolution monitoring and evidence-based management of coastal and inland aquatic ecosystems, while tightening the coupling between science, restoration prioritization, and climate policy.

82_2019.description: This body of work delineates the biological and epidemiological determinants of skeletal muscle function across the lifespan and in sport, and evaluates interventions including periodized resistance training, load monitoring, and protein supplementation to enhance performance, reduce injury, and mitigate muscle wasting and its adverse outcomes.

82_2020.description: Collectively, these studies elucidate the mechanisms, epidemiology, and clinical management of age-related skeletal muscle loss and dysfunction, detailing motor unit and satellite cell alterations, immune–endocrine and metabolic signaling mapped by multi-omics, validating grip strength as a prognostic measure, and appraising exercise and emerging pharmacotherapies to mitigate disability and adverse outcomes.

82_2021.description: This body of work delineates the mechanisms, assessment, epidemiology, and management of age- and disease-related muscle decline, integrating multi-omic and physiological insights with functional biomarkers, standardized surveillance, and evidence-based training methodologies to optimize health and performance.

82_2022.description: This collection elucidates skeletal muscle biology and its systemic consequences by integrating mechanisms of growth and wasting, exercise-induced interorgan signaling, stem cell–mediated regeneration, and age- and disease-related degeneration with epidemiology, standardized injury surveillance, multi-omic atlases, and biomarker- and technology-enabled interventions.

82_2023.description: This collection delineates a multi-scale understanding of skeletal muscle across health and disease by standardizing participant classification, elucidating molecular and stem cell mechanisms of adaptation and wasting, mapping aging with single-cell atlases, characterizing injury and sarcopenia epidemiology and assessment, and identifying actionable targets and tools—from exerkine signaling and branched-chain amino acid metabolism to AI-enabled monitoring—for precision interventions.

82_2024.description: This body of work delineates the mechanisms, measurement, and clinical impact of skeletal muscle adaptation and wasting across exercise, aging, and disease—spanning exercise-induced circulating mediators, endurance training dynamics, multi-omic atlases, and the epidemiology and diagnosis of sarcopenia and cachexia—to identify therapeutic avenues such as targeting branched-chain amino acid metabolism and GDF15 and to inform cardiometabolic care, oncology, and sports injury prevention.

82_2025.description: This body of work synthesizes epidemiology, mechanisms, and clinical implications of skeletal muscle decline across aging and chronic disease by combining single-cell and multi-omic atlases with physiological and interventional studies to define drivers such as anabolic–catabolic imbalance, neuromuscular remodeling, disrupted branched-chain amino acid catabolism, and endocrine mediators like GDF15, while advancing assessment and management via functional metrics, exercise adaptation profiling, and emerging therapeutic targets in settings including cardiovascular disease, cancer cachexia, and medically induced weight loss.
82_2025.report:
Here is a concise synthesis across the listed 2025 papers.

1) What is being researched
- Burden, risk factors, and consequences of sarcopenia across general and disease populations, including cardiovascular disease and cancer.
- Molecular and cellular mechanisms of muscle wasting and adaptation: aging, cachexia, exercise metabolism, endocrine regulators, and nutrient pathways.
- High-resolution atlases of aging human skeletal muscle to define cell-type shifts, stem cell states, neuromuscular junction changes, and chromatin programs underlying sarcopenia.
- Diagnostics and screening: sarcopenic obesity recognition, handgrip strength as a health biomarker, and practical clinical workflows.
- Therapeutic targets and strategies: metabolic pathway modulation (e.g., branched-chain amino acid catabolism), GDF15 signaling, and multi-modal interventions; assessment of drug- and diet-induced weight loss effects on lean mass.
- Digital and AI-enabled monitoring of exercise stress and workload using wearable sensors and machine learning.

2) Key methods
- Evidence synthesis: systematic review/meta-analysis, narrative reviews.
- Single-cell and single-nucleus profiling (RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, multiome) across ages and fitness/frailty strata; cross-species comparisons.
- Multi-omics for mechanism and adaptation: transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, epigenomics; temporal profiling of endurance training.
- Integrative causal inference and target nomination in omics (e.g., pathway-centric analyses implicating BCAA catabolism); genetic cross-referencing to identify chromatin features linked to sarcopenia susceptibility.
- Clinical phenotyping and functional measures: handgrip dynamometry; recognition of limitations of BMI and emphasis on muscle function.
- Preclinical models and clinical trial aggregation in cancer cachexia.
- Wearables and AI/ML (e.g., LSTM) for real-time stress/pressure estimation during sport.

3) Emerging trends (important)
- Shift from muscle mass to function and quality: sarcopenic obesity highlighted; grip strength positioned as a practical “vital sign.”
- High-resolution human muscle atlases as foundational references for aging biology, revealing niche, neuromuscular junction, immune, and stem cell state remodeling with age.
- Time-resolved, multi-omic maps of exercise training to guide personalized exercise prescriptions and mechanistic interventions.
- Metabolic pathway targeting in sarcopenia/cachexia: disrupted BCAA catabolism as a causal axis; expanding interest in endocrine/myokine regulators (e.g., GDF15) that modulate muscle energy expenditure.
- Multi-organ, immunometabolic framing of cancer cachexia with combination-therapy logic, though effective approved drugs are still lacking.
- Clinical translation focus: early screening in chronic disease (CVD, CKD, cancer), lean-mass preservation during medically induced weight loss, and pragmatic workflows beyond BMI.
- Digital biomarkers and AI-driven monitoring in sports and rehabilitation to quantify load, stress, and recovery, enabling precision training.

4) What is new
- Epidemiology of Sarcopenia: Updated global prevalence (≈10–16% in older adults) and notably higher rates in clinical populations (e.g., up to 66% in unresectable esophageal cancer). Consolidates links to mortality, complications, and functional decline; flags modifiable risk factors (inactivity, malnutrition, smoking, extreme sleep duration, diabetes) but underscores need for higher-quality causal studies.
- Sarcopenia and Cardiovascular Diseases: Clarifies bidirectional risk and clinical consequences; emphasizes sarcopenic obesity and the inadequacy of BMI; outlines diagnostic approach and management principles relevant to cardiology pathways.
- Human Skeletal Muscle Aging Atlases: Large single-cell/single-nucleus and multiome datasets map aging muscle across cell types. New observations include aging-associated muscle stem cell states (reduced ribosome biogenesis, increased CCL2), expansion of neuromuscular junction–associated nuclei (possible re-innervation), fiber-type compensation (fast-to-slow adaptations), immune infiltration patterns, and chromatin elements linked to sarcopenia susceptibility; public resources enable target discovery.
- Cancer Cachexia Review: Integrates multi-organ and molecular networks governing catabolism; compiles recent trials; reiterates the therapeutic gap and points to combinatorial and pathway-specific strategies.
- GDF15 Promotes Weight Loss by Enhancing Energy Expenditure in Muscle: Positions GDF15 beyond anorexia-mediated effects, implicating muscle energy expenditure as a mechanistic route to weight loss, highlighting a potentially druggable endocrine-muscle axis.
- Multi-omic Profiling of Sarcopenia—BCAA Catabolism: Identifies impaired branched-chain amino acid catabolism as a causal mechanism and therapeutic target, elevating mitochondrial and amino acid oxidation pathways as intervention points.
- Hand Grip Strength as a Vital Sign: Strengthens case for routine grip testing with population-specific cutoffs; broad associations with chronic diseases, hospitalization, and mortality argue for adoption in primary and specialty care.
- Temporal Dynamics of Endurance Training: Multi-omic time-course designs capture staged molecular adaptations in skeletal muscle, informing optimal dosing and timing of exercise interventions.
- Muscle Matters—Medically Induced Weight Loss: Highlights implications of pharmacologic and other rapid weight-loss approaches on lean mass and muscle function, reinforcing the need for resistance training and nutrition strategies to preserve muscle during treatment.
- AI for Exercise/Fitness Stress (IPM‑EFPM): Demonstrates an integrated wearable–LSTM pipeline for real-time stress/load assessment in basketball, suggesting a blueprint for digital phenotyping of training and recovery applicable to broader sports and rehab contexts.

83_2019.description: These works collectively advance molecular simulation by delivering improved force fields and small‑molecule parametrization, rigorous electrostatics and solvation treatments, enhanced‑sampling and end‑state free‑energy methodologies, and scalable CPU/GPU software frameworks, thereby enabling accurate, efficient, and reproducible atomistic and coarse‑grained modeling of liquids, biomolecules, and materials.

83_2020.description: These studies collectively advance molecular simulation by uniting improved force-field and charge models, scalable CPU/GPU dynamics with rigorous barostat/thermostat control, enhanced sampling and end-point free-energy techniques, and semiempirical or machine-learned potentials into automated, reproducible workflows for accurate biomolecular and materials modeling.

83_2022.description: Collectively, these works advance molecular simulation by integrating automated exploration of chemical space, improved charge and solvation models, transferable classical and machine-learned force fields, rigorous enhanced-sampling and pressure–temperature control, and optimized parallel implementations into cohesive workflows that deliver accurate, scalable free-energy and dynamics predictions from small molecules to large biomolecular and materials systems.

83_2023.description: This collection of works advances predictive, scalable, and reproducible molecular and materials simulations by integrating next-generation force fields (classical, coarse-grained, and machine-learned), robust solvation and end-state free energy methodologies, enhanced sampling and conformational exploration, automated small-molecule parameterization, and high-performance GPU-enabled software ecosystems spanning atomistic to mesoscopic regimes.

83_2024.description: This collection advances molecular and materials simulation by analyzing the influence of thermodynamic coupling on liquid properties, unifying principles of enhanced sampling, updating small‑molecule parameterization and conformer exploration, introducing transferable machine‑learned force fields, integrating multi‑scale software platforms with GPU acceleration, and promoting FAIR practices to deliver more accurate, efficient, and reproducible modeling across biomolecular and condensed‑phase systems.

83_2025.description: This collection advances the computational simulation ecosystem by delivering automated small‑molecule parametrization, conformational exploration, short‑range machine‑learned potentials, GPU‑accelerated molecular dynamics, and integrated multi‑scale platforms, emphasizing accuracy, transferability, efficiency, and FAIR‑aligned reproducibility for biomolecular and materials modeling.
83_2025.report:
Cross‑cutting emerging trends (most important)
- Rapid convergence of force-field paradigms: classical, semiempirical (GFN‑xTB), and machine-learned force fields (e.g., MACE, NEP) are being combined or swapped to balance accuracy, cost, and transferability from small molecules to biomolecules and materials.
- Automation and scale: robust, low‑touch pipelines for parametrization, conformer/tautomer/protonation-state enumeration, and sampling (SwissParam, CREST) are reducing expert overhead and enabling exhaustive exploration.
- Multiscale and interoperability: smoother coupling of QM, MM, QM/MM, and ML potentials within unified platforms (AMS, CREST interfaces) and cross‑FF compatibility (CHARMM36, CGenFF paths) is becoming standard.
- GPU democratization and performance: broader GPU vendor support (NVIDIA and AMD) and fully GPU‑native MD engines (GPUMD) are pushing longer, larger, and more accurate simulations into routine practice.
- Toward first‑principles accuracy in the condensed phase: short‑range MLFFs trained on high‑level QM now reproduce gas-, liquid-, and solid‑state properties and enable peptide folding and protein simulations with explicit solvent at modest cost (MACE‑OFF).
- FAIR, reproducibility, and provenance: growing emphasis on findable, accessible, interoperable, reusable data and workflows for biomolecular simulations, with tooling (CLIs, metadata, containers) aligning around community standards.

Paper analyses

1) ambertools (AmberTools23 application note)
1. What is being researched
- A free, open-source toolkit for setting up, running, and analyzing molecular simulations in the AMBER ecosystem.
2. Key methods
- Workflow components for system building, parameterization, QM/MM setup, implicit/explicit solvent modeling, and trajectory analysis; CPU‑oriented engines and Python tooling.
3. Emerging trends
- Consolidation of end‑to‑end simulation workflows and tighter analysis/parameterization loops within open tooling.
4. What is new
- Incremental feature additions across setup, parameterization, and analysis modules in AmberTools23, improving usability and coverage (details in the application note).

2) swissparam 2023
1. What is being researched
- Automated generation of small‑molecule topologies and parameters compatible with major biomolecular force fields.
2. Key methods
- Updated MMFF‑based parametrization compatible with CHARMM36; optional CGenFF‑style parametrization via MATCH; enumeration of likely tautomers/protonation states; support for covalent ligands; web UI and CLI.
3. Emerging trends
